turns.content: My Ender 3 prints have bad stringing between features. What retraction settings should I try?

To dial in stringing on an Ender 3, I need a few details because the “right” retraction depends heavily on extruder type, filament, and temperature. Quick questions: 1) Is it a stock Bowden setup (tube from extruder to hotend) or direct drive? 2) Filament type and brand (PLA, PETG, TPU, etc.) and how old/dry it is? 3) Nozzle size and hotend (stock MK8-style, all-metal, etc.)? 4) Print temperature and speed, especially travel speed? 5) Slicer (Cura, PrusaSlicer, etc.) and current retraction distance/speed? In the meantime, for a stock Ender 3 Bowden printing PLA, a common starting point is: - Retraction distance: 5.0 mm - Retraction speed: 40 mm/s - Minimum travel for retraction: 1.0–1.5 mm - Travel speed: 150–200 mm/s (if your machine can handle it without ringing) Then tune temperature and retraction with a stringing tower.
Stock Bowden, mostly PLA. Cura slicer. Nozzle 0.4. Temp 205. Retraction currently 6.5 mm at 25 mm/s. Travel speed 120. Filament is like a month old, not dried.

Good, that narrows it down. Your retraction distance (6.5 mm) is on the high side for a stock Bowden, and your retraction speed (25 mm/s) is a bit low. High distance can sometimes worsen stringing by pulling molten filament up into cooler areas, causing oozing and inconsistent extrusion after retraction. Low speed can allow more time for ooze during the retraction move. Try this structured approach so you can isolate variables: 1) Dry/filament check (fast sanity test) - PLA can pick up moisture in a month depending on humidity. Moist PLA often shows as fine “wispy” strings and popping/clicking at the nozzle, plus rough surface. - If you can, dry it at ~45–50°C for 4–6 hours (food dehydrator is great). If you can’t, at least test with a fresh spool if available. 2) Temperature 205°C is reasonable, but some PLAs string at 205. Try: - 200°C as a test, keeping everything else the same. If you see less stringing at 200 with no under-extrusion, you’ve found easy gains. 3) Retraction tuning baseline In Cura, set: - Retraction distance: 5.0 mm - Retraction speed: 40 mm/s - Retraction extra prime amount: 0 (important—don’t compensate yet) - Minimum travel: 1.0 mm - Combing: On (Within Infill) or try “Not in Skin” to reduce travel across open areas - Z hop: Off initially (Z hop can increase stringing by creating extra time hot and oozing during lift) 4) Travel speed 120 mm/s is okay. If your Ender 3 is stable, increase travel to 150 mm/s. Faster travel reduces the time available to ooze. 5) Run a stringing test Print a stringing tower or two-post test with the above settings, then adjust: - If you still get strings: increase retraction speed first (up to 45–50 mm/s), and/or reduce temperature by 5°C. - If you see gaps or “pitting” after travel: distance is too high or speed too high; back off. Can you describe the stringing? Is it thick hair-like strands, or wispy spiderweb? And do you hear popping or see little bubbles?
It’s mostly wispy spiderweb strings, not thick. No popping noises. Prints look otherwise fine, just messy between little pillars.

Wispy “spiderweb” stringing with PLA often responds best to a small temperature drop plus better travel/retraction speed rather than pushing retraction distance higher. Given your current 6.5 mm @ 25 mm/s, I’d do this sequence: Step A: Temp sweep first (fastest win) - Print at 205, 200, 195 with the same retraction (but I’d already change it to something sensible) Actually, to avoid confounding, do: - Retraction: 5.0 mm - Speed: 40 mm/s Then run a small two-post stringing test at 205/200/195. If 195 starts to show poor layer bonding or matte/under-extruded look, go back up. Step B: Retraction speed tweak If temperature doesn’t fully fix it: - Keep distance 5.0 mm - Try speed 45 mm/s, then 50 mm/s Step C: Distance tweak (small increments) Only if needed: - 4.5 mm, 5.0 mm, 5.5 mm I’d avoid going above ~6 mm on stock Bowden unless you have a reason, because it can promote heat creep-related issues and inconsistent priming. Step D: Cura features that help - Enable “Wipe Nozzle Between Layers” is not typical; instead: - “Coasting” can help reduce end-of-line pressure. Start low: - Coasting Volume: 0.064 mm³ (or try Cura’s default small value; too much causes under-extrusion at line ends) - “Outer Wall Wipe Distance”: 0.2–0.4 mm - “Avoid Printed Parts When Traveling”: On (sometimes helps reduce string deposit across surfaces) - “Combing Mode”: “Within Infill” is a good compromise. If you tell me your Cura profile basics (layer height, print speed, wall speed), I can suggest exact values that won’t cause artifacts.
Layer height 0.2. Print speed 50. Walls 25. Infill 50. Travel 120. Combing is on “All”. Z hop off. Retraction prime extra 0. Coasting is off.

Those speeds are reasonable. A few targeted changes: 1) Combing mode “All” can cause long travels that stay inside the model and may drag ooze across internal cavities, but for stringing between separate features (like pillars), combing doesn’t always help and can sometimes increase stringing because the nozzle stays over hot plastic longer. Try: - Combing Mode: Within Infill This forces more retractions when traveling over open areas, which often reduces visible strings. 2) Travel speed Bump travel from 120 to 150 mm/s if your printer can handle it. It’s a simple way to reduce ooze time. 3) Retraction Set: - Distance: 5.0 mm - Speed: 45 mm/s (since you’re currently low at 25) - Minimum travel: 1.0 mm - Maximum retraction count: keep default unless you see issues (but ensure it’s not artificially limiting retractions; Cura sometimes defaults to 100 which is fine) - Minimum extrusion distance window: default is okay 4) Temperature Try 200°C for PLA. If your PLA is happy at 195, that’s often a sweet spot for stringing reduction. 5) Add a small wipe In Cura: - Outer Wall Wipe Distance: 0.2–0.4 mm This helps “catch” the ooze at the end of a line. 6) Consider coasting (optional) If after the above you still get stringing, enable coasting lightly: - Coasting Volume: ~0.06–0.10 mm³ - Minimum Volume Before Coasting: keep default or ~0.8 mm³ Too much coasting causes little gaps at the end of walls. Before you change too many settings at once: do one test print with these: - 200°C - 5.0 mm @ 45 mm/s - Travel 150 - Combing Within Infill - Outer Wall Wipe 0.3 mm Then tell me what changes you see: fewer strings? Any under-extrusion after travel?
I tried 200C, 5mm at 45mm/s, travel 150, combing within infill, wipe 0.3. It’s better but still has some thin strings. Also I see tiny zits on the outside walls sometimes.

Progress is good—now we can chase the remaining wisps and the zits separately (they’re often different causes). Part 1: Remaining thin strings At this point, wisps often come from: - Slightly high temperature still - Moisture (even without popping) - Residual pressure due to acceleration/jerk or nozzle pathing - Retraction not quite optimal Try these in order: A) Drop temp another 5°C - Test 195°C with the same settings. PLA often prints fine at 195, especially at 50 mm/s. If you see weaker bridging or poor layer adhesion, go back to 200. B) Increase retraction speed slightly, keep distance - Keep distance 5.0 mm - Try 50 mm/s Sometimes that last bit reduces ooze during travel. C) Small distance tweak - Try 5.5 mm at 45–50 mm/s Don’t jump high—just 0.5 mm. Part 2: Zits on the outside walls Zits (blobs) can be caused by: - Seam placement (start/stop point) - Retraction/prime behavior - Flow/pressure changes - “Power loss recovery” feature on some Ender 3 boards (can cause brief pauses) - Slicer settings like “retract at layer change” and “combing” interactions - Linear advance/pressure advance not tuned (if enabled) Let’s diagnose: 1) Are the zits aligned vertically in a line (like a seam), or random? 2) Do they happen at a consistent height or all over? 3) Are you using “Power Loss Recovery” (sometimes called “Resume Print”) and printing from SD card? In Cura, you can reduce seam zits by: - Z Seam Alignment: “Sharpest Corner” or “User Specified” - Seam Corner Preference: “Hide Seam” - Enable “Retract at Layer Change” (usually on) - Enable “Coasting” slightly (as mentioned) - Enable “Wipe” (you already did) Also, sometimes “Combing Within Infill” increases retractions; that’s okay, but we want to ensure retractions are clean. Answer those zit pattern questions and I’ll suggest the next exact tweaks.
The zits look mostly like a vertical line, so probably seam. Printing from SD card. I think power loss recovery is on by default? Not sure. Z seam is set to “Sharpest corner”.

If it’s a vertical line, that’s almost certainly the seam—normal to some degree, but you can minimize it. 1) Seam tuning in Cura - Z Seam Alignment: Sharpest Corner is fine. - Seam Corner Preference: “Hide Seam” (if available in your Cura version) - Try “Smart Hiding” if present. - If the model doesn’t have sharp corners (cylinders), consider “Random” seam to distribute tiny zits rather than stacking them. 2) Retraction at layer change Make sure: - Retract at Layer Change: On This reduces the blob when the nozzle stops and moves up. 3) Coasting (small) Since you’re still seeing wisps and you have seam blobs, a small coasting value can help both: - Enable Coasting: On - Coasting Volume: start 0.08 mm³ - Minimum Volume Before Coasting: 0.8 mm³ (or default) If you see under-extruded corners or gaps at the end of walls, reduce coasting volume. 4) Outer wall wipe distance You already set 0.3 mm. That’s good—if you enable coasting, you can keep wipe. 5) Power loss recovery / pausing On some Creality boards, power-loss recovery writes to SD frequently, causing micro-stutters that can create periodic blobs. Those blobs usually appear at regular intervals, not necessarily exactly at the seam, but they can coincide with seam points if timing matches. If you suspect it: - Watch the printer during a wall: if you see tiny pauses, that’s a clue. - You can disable power-loss recovery in firmware on some setups, or avoid it by using a different firmware build. Not always trivial, so I’d only chase this if blobs look periodic around the whole perimeter. Now for the remaining wispy strings: - Try 195°C first. - If still there, bump retraction speed to 50 mm/s. - If still there, bump distance to 5.5 mm. Do one change at a time. If you can tell me your current PLA brand (some are stringier), I can give a more confident temperature target.

It’s Hatchbox PLA. I tried 195 and it still prints fine. Stringing is a bit less but still there. Retraction speed 50 helped a little. Haven’t tried 5.5 distance yet. Coasting 0.08 reduced seam blobs but I now see slight gaps at the end of outer walls.

Great observations. The gaps at the end of outer walls mean coasting is a bit too aggressive for your flow/line width/speed combo. Adjust coasting first: - Reduce Coasting Volume from 0.08 to 0.05 mm³ Keep everything else the same. The goal is to keep the seam improvement without starving the end of the line. Now, on stringing: Since 195°C + 5.0 mm @ 50 mm/s still leaves wisps, the next mild step is indeed distance +0.5 mm. Try: - Retraction distance: 5.5 mm - Retraction speed: 50 mm/s - Temperature: 195°C - Travel: 150 mm/s - Combing: Within Infill - Wipe: 0.3 mm - Coasting: 0.05 mm³ If that reduces stringing without causing under-extrusion after travel, you’re close. Two other Cura settings that can matter a lot for wisps: 1) “Minimum Travel” for retraction If it’s too high, Cura won’t retract on short travels between pillars, causing strings. Ensure: - Minimum Travel: 1.0 mm (or even 0.8 mm) If it’s currently 1.5–2.0, lower it. 2) “Maximum Retraction Count” and “Minimum Extrusion Distance Window” If the max count is low (like 10) and you have lots of retractions, Cura may stop retracting and strings appear. Check: - Maximum Retraction Count: 100 - Minimum Extrusion Distance Window: 10 mm (default) This is a “safety” feature; too strict can cause stringing. If you want, paste your Cura retraction section values and I’ll sanity-check them.

Minimum travel was 1.5, I lowered to 1.0. Max retraction count was 10! Window 10. I changed max count to 100. Now the stringing test looks much better. Still a few hairs but way less. Is there anything else to get it super clean?

That max retraction count being 10 would absolutely cause stringing once it hits the limit—nice catch. To get “super clean,” you’re down to the last few percent, which usually comes from a mix of material condition, temperature, and motion/path choices. Here are the highest-impact remaining options, in order: 1) Filament dryness (even for PLA) Those last few hairs are often moisture-related. Hatchbox PLA is generally good, but if it’s been out a month, drying can still help. If you can dry it, do it once and see if the remaining hairs disappear. 2) Slight temperature tweak You’re already at 195°C. You can try 190°C if: - Layer adhesion is still okay - Extrusion remains consistent At 50 mm/s, some PLAs can do 190, others can’t. If you see dull, under-extruded lines or weak parts, go back to 195. 3) Retraction distance micro-tuning If you’re at 5.5 mm now and it’s good: - Try 5.8 mm (not a huge jump) Sometimes the sweet spot is narrow. Don’t go too high; above ~6.0–6.5 you may start to get inconsistent priming. 4) “Enable Retraction” on all travel moves between features You already fixed min travel and max count. Good. 5) Travel acceleration / jerk (if you’re comfortable) Faster travel and snappier travel moves reduce ooze time. But too aggressive causes ringing. If your firmware allows: - Increase travel acceleration slightly (e.g., from 500 to 800–1000 mm/s²) - Keep print acceleration moderate This is optional and more advanced; only do it if you know how to revert. 6) Post-processing (if you just need cosmetic perfection) Even with perfect settings, some PLA will leave a few angel hairs. A quick pass with: - Hot air (heat gun on low, very quick) or - A lighter carefully (quick swipe, don’t linger) will remove hairs. This is common practice for “display” parts. Before going into acceleration tuning, tell me: what’s your current retraction distance now (5.5?), and do you get any signs of under-extrusion right after a travel (like a thin start to the next line)?

Retraction distance 5.5 now. No under-extrusion after travel that I can see. I’d rather not mess with acceleration. I can dry filament though. Also, sometimes I get strings mostly on small parts with lots of little travel moves, like miniatures.

For small parts and miniatures, two extra factors become important: heat buildup and frequent tiny travels. 1) Minimum layer time / cooling Small layers can stay too hot, which increases ooze/stringing even if retraction is correct. In Cura: - Minimum Layer Time: set to 8–12 s - Enable “Lift Head” (or “Cool Head Lift”) if available, so it moves away while waiting - Fan: 100% after initial layers for PLA (unless the part needs strength more than detail) 2) Print speed for small features If you print tiny perimeters slowly, the nozzle dwells and oozes. Cura has: - “Small Feature Speed” or similar You actually want travel to be fast, but perimeters can be either slower (for detail) or sometimes a bit faster to reduce dwell. The real fix is cooling/min layer time. 3) Retraction on short travels You already lowered minimum travel to 1.0 mm. For minis, you might even go to 0.8 mm, but don’t go too low or you’ll retract constantly and increase wear and print time. 0.8 is usually safe. 4) Combing For minis, combing can create weird paths. Try: - Combing: Not in Skin This avoids traveling across visible outer surfaces, which can reduce visible string scars. 5) Drying Drying the spool often takes “almost perfect” to “clean.” If you dry it, keep your tuned settings and repeat the same test. If it improves, you’ll know moisture was contributing. If you want a “miniatures” preset, tell me your typical nozzle temp, layer height for minis (0.12? 0.16?), and whether you use a smaller nozzle.
Minis I do 0.12 layer height with same 0.4 nozzle. Temp 200 usually for detail. Speed like 35. Fan 100 after a few layers. Minimum layer time is 5 seconds right now.

For 0.12 mm minis at 35 mm/s, I’d change these specifically: 1) Temperature Detail often pushes people hotter, but for stringing you can often go cooler once flow is stable. - Try 195°C for minis as well, especially since your PLA prints fine there. 2) Minimum layer time 5 seconds is often too low for tiny cross-sections. - Set Minimum Layer Time: 10 seconds - Enable Lift Head during cooling (so it doesn’t sit and ooze on the part) 3) Retraction Your tuned retraction should still work: - 5.5 mm @ 50 mm/s For minis with many tiny travels, you can try: - Minimum travel: 0.8–1.0 mm 4) Combing - Set Combing: Not in Skin This helps avoid dragging ooze across visible surfaces. 5) Outer wall speed If you’re at 35 overall, ensure outer wall isn’t extremely slow (like 15). Very slow outer walls can increase heat soak and ooze. Something like: - Outer wall: 20–25 mm/s - Inner wall: 30–35 mm/s 6) “Print one at a time” vs “all at once” If you print multiple minis at once, each layer has more time to cool, which often reduces stringing and improves detail. If you print one tiny mini alone, layers are too fast and heat builds. If you dry the filament and apply the 10s minimum layer time + lift head, you’ll likely see a noticeable reduction in the last hairs on minis.
I usually print one mini at a time. Maybe I should print two? Also, what about “retract at layer change” for minis—on or off?

Printing two (or more) minis at once often helps because it increases the time between layers on each mini, reducing heat buildup and oozing. The tradeoff is risk: if one fails, it can affect the other, and travel between models can introduce stringing. But since you’re already tuning stringing, it’s a good tactic. Recommendations: - Print 2 minis at once, spaced a bit apart (e.g., 40–60 mm) so the nozzle has room and doesn’t bump. - Keep travel speed high (150) and retraction enabled. Retract at layer change: - Keep it ON for minis. Layer changes are frequent and the nozzle pauses; retracting helps prevent that pause from forming a blob or string. One more mini-specific setting: - “Z Hop When Retracted”: Usually OFF for stringing, but for minis with lots of moves over delicate features, a small Z hop (0.2 mm) can prevent scarring. However it can slightly increase stringing due to extra time. If you don’t have collision/scar issues, keep it OFF. If your main issue is still hairs between separated features, keep Z hop off and focus on cooling and dryness.

Makes sense. I dried the filament in a dehydrator at 45C for 6 hours. Now stringing is almost gone on the tower. Great. But I noticed occasional clicking from the extruder during retractions at 50mm/s. Is that a problem?

Occasional extruder clicking during retractions can indicate the extruder is struggling to pull/push filament fast enough, or that there’s extra resistance in the filament path. It’s not immediately dangerous, but it can lead to inconsistent extrusion and eventually wear (chewed filament). Common causes on a Bowden Ender 3: - Retraction speed too high for the stock extruder/hob grip - Bowden tube friction or a tight bend - Extruder arm tension/condition (stock plastic arm can flex/crack) - Hotend backpressure (partial clog, dirty nozzle, too low temp for the speed) - Retraction distance/speed combo causing aggressive “snap” motions What to do: 1) Reduce retraction speed slightly If 50 mm/s clicks, try: - 45 mm/s Often you’ll keep most stringing benefits without stressing the extruder. 2) Check Bowden path - Ensure the Bowden tube has smooth curves, not a sharp kink. - Verify the tube ends are cut square and seated firmly (especially at hotend coupler). 3) Extruder tension and gear - Clean the hob gear teeth of plastic dust. - Check the idler tension isn’t too loose (slipping) or too tight (crushing filament). - Inspect the extruder arm if it’s the stock plastic one—cracks are common and can cause intermittent clicking/slipping. 4) Hotend condition - If you’ve printed a while, consider doing a cold pull or replacing the nozzle if it’s worn/dirty. - Clicking can also happen if temperature is borderline low. Since you’re at 195, if you see clicking during normal extrusion too (not just retraction), consider 200. But since the clicking is during retraction, speed is the first knob. Is the clicking only during retractions, or also during steady printing/infill?
Only during retractions, not during normal extrusion. It’s the stock plastic extruder. Bowden tube is stock too.

Then it’s likely the retraction speed is just a bit aggressive for the stock extruder’s grip/compliance. I’d do: - Retraction speed: drop to 45 mm/s Keep distance 5.5 mm. Re-test. If stringing remains clean (it probably will now that the filament is dry), you’re set. Also, since you have the stock plastic extruder: keep an eye on it. Even if it’s fine today, they often develop hairline cracks near the idler pivot that cause inconsistent tension. If you ever see under-extrusion that comes and goes, that’s a prime suspect. If you want to reduce retraction “jerkiness” without losing effectiveness: - Enable “Retraction Speed” separate settings if Cura offers it: - Retraction speed 45 - Prime speed 35–40 (slightly slower prime can reduce snapping) Not all Cura versions separate these, but many do.
Cura lets me set retraction speed and prime speed separately. Prime is currently same as retract. Should I set prime slower?

Yes—setting prime a bit slower can reduce the “thunk” and clicking when it pushes filament back in, especially with a stock extruder and Bowden elasticity. Try: - Retraction speed: 45 mm/s - Prime speed: 35 mm/s If you notice small gaps right after travel (under-priming), increase prime speed to 40. If you notice tiny blobs at the start of a line (over-pressure), keep it at 35 or even 30. Keep distance 5.5 mm for now.
After changing to retract 45, prime 35, clicking is gone. Stringing is still really good. I still get a few hairs on PETG though. Different spool.

PETG is inherently more prone to stringing than PLA, and it responds differently. For PETG, the strategy is usually: - Lower retraction distance than PLA (to avoid pulling molten PETG into the heat break) - Slightly slower retraction speed - Lower temperature if possible - Avoid aggressive cooling (depends on part) - Use higher travel speed and good wiping Questions for PETG: 1) Print temp and bed temp? 2) PETG brand? 3) Are you using the same Bowden and 0.4 nozzle? 4) What retraction settings did you use on PETG (same as PLA)?
Same hardware. PETG is Overture. I print 240 nozzle, 80 bed. I used same retraction 5.5, retract 45, prime 35. Travel 150.

For PETG on a Bowden Ender 3, 5.5 mm is often too much and can actually make stringing worse (and can cause clogs) because PETG is “sticky” and tends to form thin threads easily. Try this PETG baseline: - Temperature: 235°C (if layer adhesion remains good) - Retraction distance: 4.0 mm - Retraction speed: 30–35 mm/s - Prime speed: 25–30 mm/s - Travel: 170–200 mm/s if stable - Combing: Within Infill (or Off, depending on model; for PETG sometimes Off gives more predictable retractions) - Fan: 30–50% (varies; too much fan can hurt adhesion, but too little can increase stringing and droop) Also: - Turn OFF coasting for PETG initially. Coasting can cause gaps and PETG doesn’t always respond well. - Keep wipe (0.3–0.5) if it helps. Then run a PETG stringing test. If you still have hairs: - Drop temp another 5°C (to 230) - Increase retraction distance slightly (4.5) But I’d avoid going back to 5.5 for PETG.
I tried 235C, 4mm, retract 35, prime 25, travel 180, fan 40. Much less stringing, but now I see occasional little blobs where it starts an outer wall.

PETG loves to blob at starts/stops because it oozes and tends to leave a “booger” at the seam. A few ways to reduce start blobs: 1) Seam placement - Put the seam where it’s less visible: “Sharpest Corner” or “Back” (depending on Cura options). - For cylinders, consider “Random” to avoid a single vertical scar, but it will distribute tiny zits. 2) Slightly reduce prime / pressure at start Since you already set a slower prime, you can: - Reduce prime speed a bit more (e.g., 20–22 mm/s) or - Slightly reduce flow for outer wall (e.g., Outer Wall Flow 95–98%) Be careful: too low causes under-extrusion. 3) Wipe and retraction timing - Keep outer wall wipe distance 0.3–0.5 mm. - Ensure “Retract at layer change” is ON. 4) Temperature If you can go to 230°C without hurting strength: - Lower temp reduces ooze and seam blobs. 5) “Linear/Pressure advance” (advanced) If you’re using stock firmware, probably not enabled; ignore for now. But pressure control is the “real” fix for seam blobbing. Try the simplest first: lower temp to 230 and reduce prime speed to 20–22. If that causes gaps at the start of walls, bump prime speed back up slightly.
Lowering to 230 helped. Prime speed 22 seems okay. Still a bit of seam but acceptable. One more question: Do I need to recalibrate e-steps or flow for stringing?

E-steps and flow don’t usually directly fix stringing, but they affect pressure and extrusion consistency, which can influence oozing and seam artifacts. Here’s how to think about it: - E-steps (extruder steps/mm): This is a mechanical calibration so that when you command 100 mm, it actually feeds 100 mm. If it’s off significantly, you can get over-extrusion (more pressure, more ooze) or under-extrusion. It’s worth calibrating once, especially on a stock Ender 3. - Flow (extrusion multiplier): This is material/profile-specific fine tuning. Over-extrusion can increase stringing and blobs because the nozzle is over-pressurized. If you’re slightly over-extruding, reducing flow from 100% to 95–98% can noticeably reduce zits and strings. So: you don’t “calibrate flow for stringing” per se, but if you are over-extruding, correcting it helps. Suggested process: 1) Calibrate E-steps (once) - Heat nozzle (so filament can move) - Mark filament, extrude 100 mm, measure actual movement - Adjust steps/mm accordingly 2) Calibrate flow for each filament - Print a single-wall cube with known line width - Measure wall thickness and adjust flow so measured thickness matches expected If your prints look dimensionally okay and walls aren’t overly thick/shiny, you may already be close. But if you want that last bit of cleanliness, verifying you’re not over-extruding is worthwhile.
I think I’m slightly over-extruding because top layers look a bit rough sometimes. How much should I reduce flow?

Don’t guess too big—small changes matter. I’d do it systematically: 1) Quick check (low effort) - Reduce flow to 98% and print a small test (like a calibration cube or the same stringing test). If top surfaces improve and dimensions remain fine, you can keep it. 2) If still rough - Try 97%, then 96%. I rarely need to go below ~95% for PLA unless e-steps are off. Better method (recommended): - Do a single-wall calibration: - Set wall line count = 1 - Top layers = 0 - Infill = 0 - Print a 20x20 mm cube - Use your normal line width (often 0.4 or 0.42) - Measure wall thickness with calipers in several spots Expected thickness ≈ line width. If you measure 0.44 when expecting 0.40, you’re over-extruding ~10%, so flow should be ~90% (but that’s a big difference; usually it’s smaller). More commonly you’ll see like 0.42 vs 0.40 -> flow ~95%. If you tell me what line width you use and what thickness you measured (if you can measure), I can tell you the exact flow multiplier to set.
Line width is 0.4. I printed a single wall cube and measured about 0.42-0.43 in a few spots.

That’s about 5–7.5% over target. Compute approximate flow: - If measured 0.42 vs target 0.40: 0.40/0.42 ≈ 0.952 → 95% - If measured 0.43 vs 0.40: 0.40/0.43 ≈ 0.930 → 93% Because measurements vary and wall thickness isn’t perfectly reliable (printer dynamics, caliper pressure, etc.), I’d split the difference conservatively: - Set flow to 95% for that PLA and reprint the single-wall cube. If it comes out around 0.40–0.41, you’re good. If it’s still 0.42, drop to 94%. If it undershoots (0.38–0.39), go back up. Do this per filament type: PLA and PETG often need different flows.
After setting PLA flow to 95%, single wall is around 0.405-0.41. Top surfaces look nicer too. Stringing is basically gone. I think I’m good. Can you summarize the final settings for PLA and PETG?

PLA (Hatchbox, dried) on stock Ender 3 Bowden, Cura: - Nozzle temp: 195°C (use 200°C if you need more strength) - Bed: whatever you normally use for PLA (often 50–60°C) - Retraction distance: 5.5 mm - Retraction speed: 45 mm/s - Prime speed: 35 mm/s - Minimum travel for retraction: 1.0 mm (or 0.8 for minis) - Maximum retraction count: 100 - Retraction window: 10 mm - Travel speed: 150 mm/s (higher if stable) - Combing: Within Infill (or Not in Skin for minis) - Outer wall wipe distance: ~0.3 mm - Coasting: optional; if used, keep small (around 0.05 mm³) and watch for gaps - Flow: 95% (based on your single-wall measurement) - For minis: Minimum layer time 10 s + lift head during cooling; consider printing 2 at once PETG (Overture) on same hardware: - Nozzle temp: 230°C (adjust 230–240 based on layer adhesion) - Bed: 80°C (as you used) - Retraction distance: 4.0 mm - Retraction speed: 35 mm/s - Prime speed: ~22 mm/s - Travel speed: ~180 mm/s - Fan: ~40% (adjust for bridging/detail vs strength) - Wipe: 0.3–0.5 mm - Coasting: Off initially (PETG can gap easily) - Seam: place to a corner/back; accept a small seam as normal for PETG If you want, share a screenshot of your Cura retraction and travel sections, and I can sanity-check that nothing else is fighting these settings (like “retraction minimum travel” hidden overrides or experimental features).
I have a BME280 sensor and an Arduino Uno. How do I wire it up and read temperature data?

You can use the BME280 over I2C (simplest wiring) with the Arduino Uno. Wiring (I2C): - BME280 VCC -> Arduino 3.3V (preferred) or 5V only if your breakout explicitly supports 5V - BME280 GND -> Arduino GND - BME280 SCL -> Arduino A5 (SCL) - BME280 SDA -> Arduino A4 (SDA) Notes: - Many BME280 breakout boards include a regulator + level shifting, so they can accept 5V. Some do not. If yours is a bare module, it needs 3.3V logic. - The BME280 can have I2C address 0x76 or 0x77 depending on the SDO pin. Software: 1) Install the “Adafruit BME280 Library” and “Adafruit Unified Sensor” via Library Manager. 2) Use a sketch like: ```cpp #include <Wire.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; // I2C void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} if (!bme.begin(0x76)) { // try 0x77 if this fails Serial.println("Could not find a valid BME280 sensor!"); while (1); } } void loop() { float t = bme.readTemperature(); // Celsius float p = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; // hPa float h = bme.readHumidity(); // % Serial.print("T = "); Serial.print(t); Serial.print(" C, "); Serial.print("P = "); Serial.print(p); Serial.print(" hPa, "); Serial.print("H = "); Serial.print(h); Serial.println(" %"); delay(2000); } ``` If it doesn’t detect, the first thing to do is run an I2C scanner sketch to confirm the address. Want to share which exact BME280 board you have (photo or link), and whether it’s labeled 3.3V/5V?
The board says “BME280 GY-BME280-3.3”. It has pins: VCC, GND, SCL, SDA, CSB, SDO. No regulator that I can see. I’m guessing it’s 3.3V only?

Yes—“GY-BME280-3.3” is typically a 3.3V module without 5V-tolerant level shifting. Treat it as 3.3V-only for both power and logic. Good news: I2C on an Arduino Uno can still work safely if the pull-ups on the I2C lines go to 3.3V (not 5V). The Uno’s I2C pins are open-drain style, so the critical part is what voltage the SDA/SCL lines get pulled up to. Two common situations: 1) Your BME280 module already has pull-up resistors to VCC (3.3V). - Then SDA/SCL will idle at 3.3V and you’re safe. - Wire it as: - VCC -> 3.3V - GND -> GND - SCL -> A5 - SDA -> A4 - CSB -> 3.3V (forces I2C mode) - SDO -> GND (address 0x76) or 3.3V (address 0x77) 2) The Uno (or another I2C device) has pull-ups to 5V - Then SDA/SCL will idle at 5V and that can damage the BME280. - Fix: remove/disable 5V pull-ups, or add a proper level shifter (BSS138-based) or use an I2C level shifter board. How to check quickly: - If you have a multimeter, power the sensor from 3.3V, connect GND, SDA, SCL, and then measure SDA to GND when idle. If it’s ~3.3V, you’re good. If it’s ~5V, you have 5V pull-ups somewhere. Also: Set CSB high (to VCC) for I2C. If CSB is left floating, it can act weird. Want an I2C scanner sketch to confirm it’s seen at 0x76/0x77?
Yes please. Also I don’t have a multimeter yet. Can I still do it safely somehow?

You can still proceed cautiously. First, the I2C scanner sketch: ```cpp #include <Wire.h> void setup() { Wire.begin(); Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} Serial.println("
I2C Scanner"); } void loop() { byte error, address; int nDevices = 0; Serial.println("Scanning..."); for (address = 1; address < 127; address++) { Wire.beginTransmission(address); error = Wire.endTransmission(); if (error == 0) { Serial.print("I2C device found at 0x"); if (address < 16) Serial.print("0"); Serial.println(address, HEX); nDevices++; } } if (nDevices == 0) Serial.println("No I2C devices found
"); else Serial.println("Done
"); delay(2000); } ``` Safety without a multimeter: - If you connect ONLY the BME280 module to the Uno and nothing else, and you do NOT add external pull-up resistors to 5V, you’re often okay because many Uno boards do not have strong onboard pull-ups to 5V on SDA/SCL (they may have weak internal pull-ups, but those are around 20–50k and typically not a big issue). The bigger risk is external I2C devices or sensor shields that have 4.7k pull-ups to 5V. - So: keep the setup minimal: Uno + BME280 only. Recommended wiring for your module: - VCC -> 3.3V - GND -> GND - SDA -> A4 - SCL -> A5 - CSB -> 3.3V - SDO -> GND (start with address 0x76) Then run the scanner. If it finds 0x76 or 0x77, you’re communicating. If you want a more robust approach without measuring: - Buy a cheap 4-channel I2C level shifter module (BSS138 type). It’s a few wires and removes worry. - Or use a microcontroller that runs at 3.3V logic (like an Arduino-compatible 3.3V board), but that’s more of a platform change. Once you confirm the address, you can use the BME280 library with that address.
I wired it like you said and ran the scanner. It prints “No I2C devices found”. What should I check?

Work through these in order; most “no devices found” issues are wiring/mode/address. 1) Pin mapping on the Uno - SDA is A4, SCL is A5 on the Uno (also duplicated on the SDA/SCL pins near AREF on some boards). - Ensure SDA from sensor goes to A4 and SCL to A5 (not swapped). 2) Power and ground - Confirm VCC is on 3.3V (not 5V). - Confirm GND is connected to Uno GND. 3) Force I2C mode - CSB must be HIGH for I2C on many BME280 modules. Tie CSB to 3.3V. - SDO sets address: - SDO to GND => 0x76 - SDO to 3.3V => 0x77 If SDO is floating, the address may be unpredictable. Tie it explicitly. 4) Try both addresses in the BME280 library Even if scanner isn’t finding it, later tests can help. But scanner should find it if wiring is correct. 5) Breadboard / jumper issues - Try different jumpers. - If using a breadboard, ensure the sensor pins are actually making contact (some modules have thin pins that don’t grip well). 6) Check that your module is actually BME280, not BMP280 Some boards are mislabeled. The scanner wouldn’t care though—it would still show an address if the chip is alive. 7) I2C clock speed Rare, but try forcing standard 100kHz: ```cpp Wire.begin(); Wire.setClock(100000); ``` 8) Sensor orientation/pin order Some GY-BME280 boards have the pin labels on the back, or the header is rotated. Double-check that the pin you think is VCC is actually VCC. If you can, describe exactly how the pins are arranged on your board (left-to-right as you read the text) and how you connected them. A photo helps too, but you can also just list “board pin -> Uno pin”.
The pins on the board left to right are: VCC GND SCL SDA CSB SDO. I connected VCC to 3.3V, GND to GND, SCL to A5, SDA to A4, CSB to 3.3V, SDO to GND. Still nothing.

That wiring sounds correct. Next likely causes: the module isn’t powered correctly (3.3V pin not actually providing power), bad connection, or a dead/mislabeled board. Let’s do two quick tests. Test A: Verify the Uno 3.3V pin is working - The Uno’s 3.3V output is limited (often ~50 mA). The BME280 draws only a tiny amount, so that’s fine. - But if you accidentally shorted 3.3V earlier, it can sag. Without a multimeter it’s hard, but you can do a crude check: - Temporarily power the sensor from 5V ONLY if your board has a regulator (yours likely doesn’t), so don’t do that. Instead: - If you have any known 3.3V device (like an ESP module), you could check it powers, but you probably don’t. Test B: Try using the SPI interface as a sanity check Your module exposes CSB/SDO, so it supports SPI too. SPI uses different pins and doesn’t depend on I2C pull-ups. If SPI works, your sensor is alive and the issue is I2C wiring/mode. For SPI wiring (Uno): - VCC -> 3.3V - GND -> GND - SCK (on sensor: SCL pin doubles as SCK in SPI) -> D13 - SDI (on sensor: SDA pin doubles as MOSI) -> D11 - SDO -> D12 (MISO) - CSB -> D10 (chip select) In SPI mode, CSB is controlled by the Arduino, not tied high. So move CSB from 3.3V to D10. Then use an SPI BME280 example (Adafruit library supports SPI). Example: ```cpp #include <SPI.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #define BME_CS 10 Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS); // hardware SPI void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} if (!bme.begin()) { Serial.println("BME280 not found via SPI"); while (1); } } void loop() { Serial.println(bme.readTemperature()); delay(1000); } ``` If SPI also fails, suspect: - bad power/ground connection - wrong pin mapping (module labels not matching) - defective module Before jumping to SPI, one more I2C-specific check: - Try connecting SDA/SCL to the dedicated SDA/SCL pins on the Uno (if your board has them) instead of A4/A5. They’re electrically the same, but you may have a bad header contact on A4/A5. Do you want to try SPI, or do you have the SDA/SCL pins available near AREF?
I tried the SDA/SCL pins near AREF and still no device. Let’s try SPI. But I’m confused: the sensor only has SCL and SDA labeled. Those become SCK and MOSI?

Correct. On many BME280 breakouts, the pins are labeled for I2C (SCL/SDA), but the chip supports both I2C and SPI, so those same pins serve dual purposes: - In I2C mode: - SCL = I2C clock - SDA = I2C data - In SPI mode: - SCL pin becomes SCK (SPI clock) - SDA pin becomes SDI/MOSI (data into the sensor) And: - SDO is MISO (data out from the sensor) - CSB is chip select (active low) SPI wiring recap (Uno): - BME VCC -> Uno 3.3V - BME GND -> Uno GND - BME SCL -> Uno D13 (SCK) - BME SDA -> Uno D11 (MOSI) - BME SDO -> Uno D12 (MISO) - BME CSB -> Uno D10 (CS) Make sure you remove the CSB->3.3V connection; CSB should be controlled by D10 now. Then use the SPI sketch. If it still doesn’t respond, try a different CS pin (like D9) and update `#define BME_CS 9`, in case D10 is being used by something else (usually not, but worth trying). Also: keep wires short for SPI, especially on a breadboard. After you upload, open Serial Monitor at 9600. If it says “BME280 not found via SPI”, we’ll go deeper (library init parameters, SPI mode, etc.).
SPI worked! It prints temperature values. So I2C was the problem. I’d prefer I2C though because I might add an OLED later. How can I make I2C work safely with the Uno?

Since SPI proves the sensor is alive, the I2C failure is almost certainly pull-ups/voltage level or I2C mode/address pin state. To make I2C work reliably with a 3.3V-only BME280 on a 5V Uno, the cleanest solution is an I2C level shifter. Option 1 (recommended): Use a BSS138-based bidirectional I2C level shifter module Wiring: - Uno 5V -> HV - Uno GND -> GND - Uno SDA (A4) -> HV1 (or “SDA_H”) - Uno SCL (A5) -> HV2 (or “SCL_H”) - Uno 3.3V -> LV - BME280 GND -> GND (common ground with Uno) - BME280 SDA -> LV1 (“SDA_L”) - BME280 SCL -> LV2 (“SCL_L”) Then: - BME280 VCC -> 3.3V - BME280 CSB -> 3.3V - BME280 SDO -> GND (0x76) or 3.3V (0x77) This ensures the pull-ups on each side go to the correct voltage. Option 2: Ensure pull-ups to 3.3V and no 5V pull-ups exist This can work without a level shifter, but it’s easy to get wrong if you later add other I2C devices that pull up to 5V. - You’d add (typically) 4.7k pull-ups from SDA to 3.3V and SCL to 3.3V - And ensure nothing pulls them to 5V Since you want to add an OLED later: many OLED modules (SSD1306) are happy on 3.3V I2C signals even if powered at 5V, but some have 5V pull-ups onboard. That can break things again. A level shifter avoids surprises. One more detail: On your current I2C attempt, you tied CSB high and SDO low, which is correct. So the likely reason it didn’t scan is the I2C lines were sitting at 5V due to pull-ups. The BME280 then wouldn’t respond (or could be stressed). SPI worked because it’s push-pull and you were likely driving 5V logic into a 3.3V chip, which is actually also not ideal. It worked, but long-term it’s safer to level shift for SPI too, or ensure the breakout has series resistors/level shifting (many don’t). Given that: I’d strongly recommend a level shifter either way, or switch to a breakout that includes regulator + level shifting. Do you want a parts list for a weather station build (level shifter, OLED, maybe RTC, SD logging), and how to structure the code cleanly?
Yes, parts list and code structure would be great. I want temperature, humidity, pressure, maybe light level. Display on OLED, and log to SD card. I’m a beginner though.

Parts list (beginner-friendly, common modules): Core: - Arduino Uno (you have) - BME280 sensor module (3.3V-only in your case) - I2C bidirectional level shifter (BSS138 type, 4-channel) - OLED display: SSD1306 128x64 I2C module - MicroSD card module (SPI interface, typically 5V-tolerant but check) - Optional: BH1750 light sensor (I2C, simple lux readings) or TEMT6000 analog light sensor Power/connection: - Breadboard + jumper wires - A small pack of 4.7k resistors (useful for pull-ups if needed) - A multimeter (strongly recommended for debugging) - Optional: DS3231 RTC module (I2C) for accurate timestamps in logs Architecture / code structure: As a beginner, you can still keep code organized by separating concerns: 1) Sensor layer: functions to read each sensor - `readBME280()` - `readLight()` - `readRTC()` (if used) 2) Display layer: draw values on OLED - `updateDisplay(data)` 3) Logging layer: write CSV to SD card - `logToSD(data)` 4) Main loop: schedule tasks - read sensors every N seconds - update display every N seconds - log every N seconds Data structure: Create a struct to store readings: ```cpp struct Reading { float tempC; float humidity; float pressure_hPa; float lux; unsigned long ms; }; ``` Wiring overview with level shifter for I2C bus: I2C devices: BME280, OLED, BH1750, DS3231 (all on same I2C bus) - Uno SDA/SCL -> level shifter HV side - 3.3V devices (BME280, BH1750 if 3.3V) -> level shifter LV side - OLED modules vary: some are 3.3–5V safe and have pull-ups to VCC. If you power OLED at 5V and it has pull-ups to 5V, that would force I2C high to 5V. With a level shifter, that’s fine. SD card module (SPI): - Uses D10–D13 typically (CS, MOSI, MISO, SCK) - Important: SD modules often have onboard level shifting; many are designed for 5V Arduinos. If you use a bare SD socket, you must level shift. Potential pin plan: - I2C: A4/A5 (through level shifter) - SD SPI: D10 CS, D11 MOSI, D12 MISO, D13 SCK - Keep D10 as output even if not used as CS for some libraries. If you want, I can propose a specific wiring map that avoids conflicts and a starter sketch that: - reads BME280 + BH1750 - displays on SSD1306 - logs CSV to SD with timestamps (with or without RTC) Do you want to include an RTC module now, or is “milliseconds since start” okay for the first version?

Let’s include RTC, because I want real timestamps in the CSV. Can you give me a full wiring map and a starter sketch? Assume I have: Uno, BME280, SSD1306 I2C OLED, BH1750, DS3231, microSD module, and the I2C level shifter.

Wiring map (one workable approach) I2C bus (through level shifter): Uno side (5V / HV): - Uno 5V -> Level Shifter HV - Uno GND -> Level Shifter GND - Uno SDA (A4) -> Level Shifter HV1 (SDA_H) - Uno SCL (A5) -> Level Shifter HV2 (SCL_H) 3.3V / LV side: - Uno 3.3V -> Level Shifter LV - BME280 VCC -> 3.3V - BME280 GND -> GND - BME280 SDA -> Level Shifter LV1 (SDA_L) - BME280 SCL -> Level Shifter LV2 (SCL_L) - BME280 CSB -> 3.3V (I2C mode) - BME280 SDO -> GND (address 0x76) Now, OLED / BH1750 / DS3231: These modules are often okay at 5V and use I2C. The question is whether you want them on the HV side (5V I2C) or LV side (3.3V I2C). To keep it simple: - Put OLED + DS3231 + BH1750 on the HV (5V) side if they are 5V-friendly modules. - Keep BME280 alone on LV side. So connect: - OLED VCC -> 5V (or 3.3V if your OLED supports it; 5V is common) - OLED GND -> GND - OLED SDA -> Uno SDA (A4) (or to HV1) - OLED SCL -> Uno SCL (A5) (or to HV2) - DS3231 VCC -> 5V - DS3231 GND -> GND - DS3231 SDA -> A4 - DS3231 SCL -> A5 - BH1750 VCC -> 5V (many support 3.3–5V) - BH1750 GND -> GND - BH1750 SDA -> A4 - BH1750 SCL -> A5 - BH1750 ADDR pin: leave floating or tie per module (often low=0x23, high=0x5C) Important: Make sure the I2C lines are shared correctly: - HV side: Uno SDA/SCL, OLED SDA/SCL, DS3231 SDA/SCL, BH1750 SDA/SCL, and HV1/HV2 of shifter all connect together (same bus). - LV side: BME280 SDA/SCL connect to LV1/LV2 (and only other 3.3V I2C devices if you add them later). SPI bus (SD card module): - SD VCC -> 5V (most SD modules with regulator) - SD GND -> GND - SD SCK -> D13 - SD MISO -> D12 - SD MOSI -> D11 - SD CS -> D10 (or another pin, but D10 is typical) Starter sketch outline Libraries to install: - Adafruit BME280 - Adafruit Unified Sensor - Adafruit SSD1306 - Adafruit GFX - RTClib (by Adafruit) for DS3231 - BH1750 (there are several; a common one is “BH1750” by Christopher Laws) - SD (built-in) or SdFat (more robust) Here is a starter sketch using: - Adafruit_BME280 (I2C) - Adafruit_SSD1306 - RTClib - BH1750 - SD You may need to adjust OLED address (0x3C or 0x3D), and BME280 address (0x76 or 0x77). ```cpp #include <Wire.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <SD.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #include <Adafruit_GFX.h> #include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h> #include <RTClib.h> #include <BH1750.h> #define SCREEN_WIDTH 128 #define SCREEN_HEIGHT 64 #define OLED_RESET -1 Adafruit_SSD1306 display(SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT, &Wire, OLED_RESET); Adafruit_BME280 bme; // I2C RTC_DS3231 rtc; BH1750 lightMeter; const int SD_CS = 10; File logFile; struct Reading { float tempC; float humidity; float pressure_hPa; float lux; DateTime now; }; bool initSD() { if (!SD.begin(SD_CS)) return false; // Create file if it doesn't exist, write header if new if (!SD.exists("log.csv")) { logFile = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (logFile) { logFile.println("timestamp,tempC,humidity,pressure_hPa,lux"); logFile.close(); } } return true; } void logReading(const Reading &r) { logFile = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (!logFile) return; char ts[20]; // YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS snprintf(ts, sizeof(ts), "%04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d", r.now.year(), r.now.month(), r.now.day(), r.now.hour(), r.now.minute(), r.now.second()); logFile.print(ts); logFile.print(","); logFile.print(r.tempC, 2); logFile.print(","); logFile.print(r.humidity, 2); logFile.print(","); logFile.print(r.pressure_hPa, 2); logFile.print(","); logFile.println(r.lux, 1); logFile.close(); } void showReading(const Reading &r) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0, 0); display.print(r.now.year()); display.print("-"); if (r.now.month() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.month()); display.print("-"); if (r.now.day() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.day()); display.setCursor(0, 10); if (r.now.hour() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.hour()); display.print(":"); if (r.now.minute() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.minute()); display.print(":"); if (r.now.second() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.second()); display.setCursor(0, 24); display.print("T: "); display.print(r.tempC, 1); display.println(" C"); display.setCursor(0, 34); display.print("H: "); display.print(r.humidity, 1); display.println(" %"); display.setCursor(0, 44); display.print("P: "); display.print(r.pressure_hPa, 1); display.println(" hPa"); display.setCursor(0, 54); display.print("L: "); display.print(r.lux, 0); display.println(" lx"); display.display(); } Reading readAll() { Reading r; r.now = rtc.now(); r.tempC = bme.readTemperature(); r.humidity = bme.readHumidity(); r.pressure_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; r.lux = lightMeter.readLightLevel(); return r; } void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); Wire.begin(); // OLED if (!display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, 0x3C)) { Serial.println("OLED init failed"); while (1); } display.clearDisplay(); display.display(); // RTC if (!rtc.begin()) { Serial.println("RTC not found"); while (1); } if (rtc.lostPower()) { Serial.println("RTC lost power, setting time to compile time"); rtc.adjust(DateTime(F(__DATE__), F(__TIME__))); } // BME280 if (!bme.begin(0x76)) { Serial.println("BME280 not found at 0x76, trying 0x77"); if (!bme.begin(0x77)) { Serial.println("BME280 init failed"); while (1); } } // BH1750 if (!lightMeter.begin(BH1750::CONTINUOUS_HIGH_RES_MODE)) { Serial.println("BH1750 init failed"); while (1); } // SD pinMode(SD_CS, OUTPUT); if (!initSD()) { Serial.println("SD init failed"); // You can choose to continue without SD: // while (1); } else { Serial.println("SD init ok"); } Serial.println("Weather station started"); } unsigned long lastSampleMs = 0; const unsigned long sampleIntervalMs = 2000; // 2s for testing void loop() { unsigned long nowMs = millis(); if (nowMs - lastSampleMs >= sampleIntervalMs) { lastSampleMs = nowMs; Reading r = readAll(); showReading(r); logReading(r); Serial.print("T="); Serial.print(r.tempC); Serial.print(" H="); Serial.print(r.humidity); Serial.print(" P="); Serial.print(r.pressure_hPa); Serial.print(" L="); Serial.print(r.lux); Serial.println(); } } ``` A few practical notes: - If the OLED address isn’t 0x3C, scan I2C and change it to 0x3D. - If BH1750 address differs, the library usually handles it, but some require specifying address. - If SD fails, it can be due to wiring or the module expecting 3.3V; many modules are 5V-ready. If you run the I2C scanner again with the level shifter installed, you should see addresses for OLED, RTC, BH1750, and BME280 (through the shifter). Want help interpreting the scanner output and mapping each address to a device?
I got the level shifter and now the I2C scanner sees devices at 0x3C, 0x57, 0x68, and 0x76. Which is which? Also the sketch runs but lux is always 54612 or something huge.

Address mapping: - 0x3C is almost certainly the SSD1306 OLED. - 0x68 is the DS3231 RTC. - 0x76 is the BME280 (since you tied SDO to GND). - 0x57 is commonly an EEPROM on some RTC modules (AT24C32/AT24C64) that rides along with DS3231 boards. Many DS3231 breakout boards include a small EEPROM at 0x57. Lux reading huge (like 54612) suggests the BH1750 isn’t being read correctly, or the value is uninitialized/overflowing, or the sensor isn’t actually a BH1750 (or not powered / wrong address / wrong mode). Things to check: 1) BH1750 address and wiring - Some BH1750 modules default to address 0x23 (ADDR low) or 0x5C (ADDR high). Your scanner output did not show 0x23 or 0x5C, which is suspicious. You saw 0x57, 0x68, 0x76, 0x3C only. So the BH1750 may not be present on the bus at all. If it’s not detected: - Check BH1750 wiring: VCC, GND, SDA, SCL. - Ensure it’s on the HV side (5V I2C) if powered with 5V, or on LV side if powered with 3.3V. Either can work, but be consistent. - If you accidentally put BH1750 on LV side while powering it at 5V (or vice versa), it can fail. 2) Library begin() result In the sketch I gave, it does: `if (!lightMeter.begin(...)) { ... }` Depending on the BH1750 library, `begin()` may always return true even if not found. Some versions don’t detect presence. 3) Try a dedicated BH1750 test sketch This reduces interactions. Example: ```cpp #include <Wire.h> #include <BH1750.h> BH1750 lightMeter; void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); Wire.begin(); Serial.println("BH1750 test"); lightMeter.begin(BH1750::CONTINUOUS_HIGH_RES_MODE); } void loop() { float lux = lightMeter.readLightLevel(); Serial.print("Lux: "); Serial.println(lux); delay(1000); } ``` But first: rerun the scanner with BH1750 connected and confirm you see 0x23 or 0x5C. If you don’t, it’s not communicating. 4) If your BH1750 module has an “ADDR” pad/pin - Tie it to GND for 0x23 (default) or to VCC for 0x5C. Some modules float in a weird state; better to tie explicitly. Given your scanner output, I’d focus on getting BH1750 to show up as an I2C device first. How did you wire BH1750 (VCC and which side of the shifter), and does it have an ADDR pin/pad?
I wired BH1750 VCC to 5V, GND to GND, SDA to A4, SCL to A5 directly. It has ADDR pin and I left it unconnected. Still not showing in scanner, but the OLED and RTC and BME show up.

If BH1750 isn’t showing in the scanner, it’s not on the bus (or it’s faulty). Leaving ADDR floating can definitely cause issues. Tie it explicitly. Do this: - Connect BH1750 ADDR -> GND (for address 0x23). Use a jumper wire. Then rerun the I2C scanner. You should see 0x23 appear. If still not detected: 1) Swap SDA/SCL (easy mistake) 2) Try powering BH1750 from 3.3V instead of 5V (many modules support both). If you do that, keep SDA/SCL on the same I2C bus (HV side is fine because I2C lines are shared and pulled up to 5V—however, if the module is 3.3V-only, it won’t like 5V I2C). If uncertain, you can put BH1750 on the LV side of the shifter and power it from 3.3V, which is safest. Given you already have a shifter, a conservative approach is: - BH1750 VCC -> 3.3V - BH1750 GND -> GND - BH1750 SDA/SCL -> LV side (same as BME280), or use spare channels on the shifter if you want to keep buses separate (but easiest is just share LV SDA/SCL). But note: then the BH1750 will appear on the LV I2C bus; the Uno still sees it through the shifter. 3) Check module pin labels—some BH1750 boards are labeled “SCL/SDA” but the header orientation can be confusing. 4) Try shorter wires; I2C can be finicky with long jumpers. Once it shows up as 0x23 or 0x5C, the lux readings should become sane (typical indoor: ~50–500 lux; bright sunlight: tens of thousands). Next step: tie ADDR to GND and scan again. What addresses do you see after that?
After tying ADDR to GND, scanner shows 0x23 now. Great. Lux values now look realistic. Next: I want to compute altitude from pressure and show it. How do I do that?

You can estimate altitude from pressure using the barometric formula, but you need a reference sea-level pressure (often called “QNH”). Without that, altitude will be off because weather changes pressure. The Adafruit_BME280 library has a helper: - `bme.readAltitude(seaLevelhPa);` Example: ```cpp float seaLevelhPa = 1013.25; // standard atmosphere float altitudeM = bme.readAltitude(seaLevelhPa); ``` Better: let the user set sea level pressure for calibration (from a nearby weather report or a known reference). For a project, you can: - Use 1013.25 hPa as a default - Provide a constant you can tweak Integrate into your Reading struct: ```cpp float altitude_m; ``` And in `readAll()`: ```cpp r.altitude_m = bme.readAltitude(1013.25); ``` Then display: ```cpp display.print("Alt: "); display.print(r.altitude_m, 1); display.println(" m"); ``` A note about “altitude” here: - It’s a derived estimate, not GPS altitude. - It will drift with weather pressure; if a storm moves in, altitude estimate can change even if you don’t move. If you want to make it more meaningful, you can instead compute “sea-level pressure” from a known altitude (if you know your approximate elevation) and then log that as “SLP”. The library also has: - `bme.seaLevelForAltitude(altitudeMeters, pressurehPa);` Which direction do you want: estimate altitude from assumed sea-level pressure, or estimate sea-level pressure from assumed altitude?
Let’s estimate altitude from assumed sea-level pressure. Also I’d like to add a simple menu to adjust seaLevelhPa using two buttons. How can I do that without making code too complex?

Keep it simple: two buttons (UP/DOWN) to adjust a global `seaLevelhPa` value, and maybe a third “MODE” button to switch between normal display and “set sea level” screen. If you only have two buttons, you can use a long-press to enter/exit setting mode. Hardware: - Two momentary buttons wired to GND and two digital pins using internal pull-ups. Example wiring: - Button UP: one side to D2, other side to GND - Button DOWN: one side to D3, other side to GND In code: - `pinMode(2, INPUT_PULLUP);` - `pinMode(3, INPUT_PULLUP);` Pressed = reads LOW. Add: - `float seaLevelhPa = 1013.25;` - `bool settingMode = false;` Debounce: Use a simple debounce by checking state changes and time. Pseudo approach: - Short press UP/DOWN: if in settingMode, adjust seaLevelhPa by +0.5 / -0.5 - Long press both together (or long press UP) toggles settingMode Here’s a compact implementation: ```cpp const int BTN_UP = 2; const int BTN_DN = 3; float seaLevelhPa = 1013.25; bool settingMode = false; unsigned long upPressStart = 0; bool upWasDown = false; unsigned long dnPressStart = 0; bool dnWasDown = false; bool readButtonPressed(int pin) { return digitalRead(pin) == LOW; } void handleButtons() { bool upDown = readButtonPressed(BTN_UP); bool dnDown = readButtonPressed(BTN_DN); unsigned long t = millis(); // Detect long press on UP to toggle mode if (upDown && !upWasDown) { upPressStart = t; upWasDown = true; } if (!upDown && upWasDown) { unsigned long dt = t - upPressStart; upWasDown = false; if (dt > 800) { settingMode = !settingMode; } else { if (settingMode) seaLevelhPa += 0.5; } } // DOWN short press adjusts when in setting mode if (dnDown && !dnWasDown) { dnPressStart = t; dnWasDown = true; } if (!dnDown && dnWasDown) { unsigned long dt = t - dnPressStart; dnWasDown = false; if (dt > 800) { // optional: long press down could also toggle mode settingMode = !settingMode; } else { if (settingMode) seaLevelhPa -= 0.5; } } // clamp to reasonable range if (seaLevelhPa < 900) seaLevelhPa = 900; if (seaLevelhPa > 1100) seaLevelhPa = 1100; } ``` Then compute altitude: ```cpp float altitudeM = bme.readAltitude(seaLevelhPa); ``` Display: - In normal mode: show altitude - In setting mode: show “Set SLP: xxxx.x hPa” and maybe also show current pressure to help calibrate. To keep code manageable, update buttons every loop, and only redraw screen at your sample interval (or faster if in setting mode). Do you want to store `seaLevelhPa` to EEPROM so it persists after reset?
Yes, store it so it persists. Also, sometimes my SD logging fails randomly after a few minutes. It stops writing. Any ideas?

EEPROM storage (persist seaLevelhPa) The Uno has built-in EEPROM. Use the EEPROM library and store a float plus a simple signature to know if it’s valid. Example: ```cpp #include <EEPROM.h> const int EEPROM_ADDR_SIG = 0; const int EEPROM_ADDR_VAL = 4; const uint32_t SIG = 0xBEEFCAFE; void saveSeaLevel() { EEPROM.put(EEPROM_ADDR_SIG, SIG); EEPROM.put(EEPROM_ADDR_VAL, seaLevelhPa); } void loadSeaLevel() { uint32_t sigRead; EEPROM.get(EEPROM_ADDR_SIG, sigRead); if (sigRead == SIG) { EEPROM.get(EEPROM_ADDR_VAL, seaLevelhPa); // sanity check if (seaLevelhPa < 900 || seaLevelhPa > 1100) seaLevelhPa = 1013.25; } else { seaLevelhPa = 1013.25; } } ``` Call `loadSeaLevel()` in setup. Call `saveSeaLevel()` when you exit setting mode (or after a change with a delay to avoid excessive writes). EEPROM has limited write cycles, so don’t write every loop. SD logging randomly stops Common causes on Uno projects: 1) Not closing files (or file handle leaks) You are closing each time, which is good. But if `SD.open()` fails and you don’t handle it, you silently stop. 2) Power instability to SD module SD cards can draw bursts of current during writes. If powered from a marginal 3.3V source or weak wiring, it can glitch. Many SD modules have regulator and run on 5V; that often helps. 3) SPI chip select conflicts If any other device shares SPI (not in your list aside from SD), ensure CS pins are set properly. Even unused SPI devices can interfere if their CS floats low. - Keep SD CS as output and set HIGH when not using. - Also ensure pin 10 is OUTPUT even if you use a different CS, because Uno uses it for SPI master mode. 4) Using long wires / poor breadboard connections on SPI SPI at default speed can be sensitive. 5) FAT filesystem / write buffering issues The standard SD library is less robust than SdFat. Also, frequent open/close can fragment or be slow. Practical improvements: A) Lower SPI speed (some SD modules/cards are picky) The built-in SD library doesn’t expose speed easily. Using SdFat gives more control, but you can try shorter wires first. B) Add `logFile.flush()` before close (usually close flushes anyway) But if you keep the file open, you must flush periodically. C) Keep the file open and flush every N lines This reduces overhead but increases risk of data loss if power is interrupted. For a bench project it’s fine. D) Add error prints When open fails, print to Serial so you know it failed rather than silently stopping. Example change: ```cpp bool logReading(const Reading &r) { File f = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (!f) { Serial.println("SD open failed"); return false; } ... f.close(); return true; } ``` E) Ensure CS pins are stable - `pinMode(10, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(10, HIGH);` before `SD.begin(10);` - If you have an OLED that uses SPI (yours is I2C, so fine), it could conflict, but it doesn’t. Questions to narrow it down: - Is your SD module powered from 5V or 3.3V? - Are you using a full-size SD module with regulator/level shifting, or a bare microSD breakout? - When it “stops writing,” does Serial still show readings? Does it print any SD error?

SD module is powered from 5V. It’s the common microSD module with a regulator and level shifters. Serial still prints readings, but SD file stops growing. No errors because I didn’t print any. I’m using long jumper wires.

Long jumpers are a prime suspect for SPI flakiness. SD cards are surprisingly timing-sensitive. Do these fixes in order: 1) Add error reporting Change your logging function to return success and print failures: ```cpp bool logReading(const Reading &r) { File f = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (!f) { Serial.println("SD open failed"); return false; } // write line... if (!f) { // stream error state Serial.println("SD write failed"); f.close(); return false; } f.close(); return true; } ``` 2) Shorten SPI wiring Keep D11/D12/D13/D10 wires short and tidy; avoid running them alongside noisy wires. 3) Ensure CS is stable and not floating In setup: ```cpp pinMode(10, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(10, HIGH); if (!SD.begin(10)) ... ``` 4) Reduce logging frequency If you’re logging every 2 seconds, that’s fine, but for long-term you might log every 30s or 60s. Frequent writes increase chances of failure and card wear. 5) Consider SdFat library SdFat is more robust and provides error codes. If the built-in SD library continues to misbehave, switching is worthwhile. 6) Power decoupling If you have capacitors, placing a 100uF electrolytic + 0.1uF ceramic near the SD module power pins can help with current spikes. Not mandatory, but helpful. 7) Avoid String usage If you build CSV lines using Arduino `String`, it can fragment SRAM over time and cause weird failures. Use `print()` calls or `snprintf` into a char buffer. In the code earlier, we mostly used `print()` and `snprintf` for timestamp, which is good. Given “after a few minutes” it stops growing: that could be a resource issue or intermittent SPI. Start by adding error prints and shortening wires. If you see “SD open failed” after some time, it might be card/module glitching. If you never see open fail but file still doesn’t grow, it might be that you’re writing to a different file (unlikely) or data isn’t flushed (but close should flush). Try this experiment: - After every write, also print a counter to serial and include it in the CSV. Then check whether the last counter in the file matches what serial printed. That tells you exactly when it stopped. Want me to rewrite the logging part to include a line counter and more explicit error handling?
Yes, please rewrite logging with a counter and avoid Strings. Also, I want CSV to include altitude and seaLevelhPa.

Here’s a logging approach that: - uses a global line counter - writes CSV with timestamp, temp, humidity, pressure, altitude, seaLevelhPa, lux - avoids Arduino String - prints explicit SD errors to Serial Add these globals: ```cpp uint32_t logLine = 0; ``` Update Reading struct: ```cpp struct Reading { float tempC; float humidity; float pressure_hPa; float altitude_m; float seaLevel_hPa; float lux; DateTime now; }; ``` Update readAll(): ```cpp Reading readAll() { Reading r; r.now = rtc.now(); r.tempC = bme.readTemperature(); r.humidity = bme.readHumidity(); r.pressure_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; r.seaLevel_hPa = seaLevelhPa; r.altitude_m = bme.readAltitude(seaLevelhPa); r.lux = lightMeter.readLightLevel(); return r; } ``` Logging init: create header if missing: ```cpp bool initSD() { if (!SD.begin(SD_CS)) { Serial.println("SD.begin failed"); return false; } if (!SD.exists("log.csv")) { File f = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (!f) { Serial.println("Failed to create log.csv"); return false; } f.println("line,timestamp,tempC,humidity,pressure_hPa,altitude_m,seaLevel_hPa,lux"); f.close(); } return true; } ``` Logging function: ```cpp bool logReading(const Reading &r) { File f = SD.open("log.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (!f) { Serial.println("SD open failed (log.csv)"); return false; } char ts[20]; snprintf(ts, sizeof(ts), "%04d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d", r.now.year(), r.now.month(), r.now.day(), r.now.hour(), r.now.minute(), r.now.second()); logLine++; f.print(logLine); f.print(","); f.print(ts); f.print(","); f.print(r.tempC, 2); f.print(","); f.print(r.humidity, 2); f.print(","); f.print(r.pressure_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.print(r.altitude_m, 2); f.print(","); f.print(r.seaLevel_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.println(r.lux, 1); if (!f) { Serial.println("SD write error (stream state bad)"); f.close(); return false; } f.close(); return true; } ``` And in loop: ```cpp bool ok = logReading(r); if (!ok) { Serial.print("Log failed at line "); Serial.println(logLine); } ``` One more improvement: load logLine from existing file Optional but nice. The SD library can’t easily seek from end and parse last line efficiently. As a beginner, simplest is to reset counter each boot. Or you can store `logLine` in EEPROM too (but that adds EEPROM writes). I’d keep it simple and reset logLine to 0 each run. If you want more resilience, consider writing to a new filename each boot (like LOG0001.CSV) to avoid appending issues and to keep files manageable. Do you want a “new file each boot” approach? It’s very common in logger projects.
New file each boot sounds good. Can you show how to auto-increment file names like LOG000.CSV?

Yes. Common pattern: try filenames LOG000.CSV through LOG999.CSV and pick the first that doesn’t exist. Add: ```cpp char logName[13]; // "LOG000.CSV" + null ``` Function to find a new name and create it with header: ```cpp bool createNewLogFile() { for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++) { snprintf(logName, sizeof(logName), "LOG%03d.CSV", i); if (!SD.exists(logName)) { File f = SD.open(logName, FILE_WRITE); if (!f) { Serial.println("Failed to create log file"); return false; } f.println("line,timestamp,tempC,humidity,pressure_hPa,altitude_m,seaLevel_hPa,lux"); f.close(); Serial.print("Logging to "); Serial.println(logName); logLine = 0; return true; } } Serial.println("No available log filename (LOG000-LOG999)"); return false; } ``` Then in setup, after `SD.begin`: ```cpp if (!SD.begin(SD_CS)) { Serial.println("SD init failed"); } else { if (!createNewLogFile()) { Serial.println("Log file create failed"); } } ``` And update `logReading` to open `logName` instead of fixed "log.csv": ```cpp File f = SD.open(logName, FILE_WRITE); ``` This is robust and avoids a single file growing forever. If you also want the OLED to show which file it’s logging to (handy), you can display `logName` in a corner. Anything else you want to add next—like averaging/filtering sensor readings, min/max tracking, or a simple serial command interface to set seaLevelhPa and time?
Let’s do min/max tracking for temperature and humidity since boot, and show them on a second screen that I can switch to with a button press.

Add a “screen index” and a button to toggle screens. Since you already have UP/DOWN, you can use a short press on UP (when not in settingMode) to toggle screens, and keep long press for entering setting mode. Or add a third button if you prefer. I’ll show a two-button approach: Behavior proposal: - Long press UP (>800 ms): toggle settingMode - Short press UP (when not settingMode): toggle screen (0=main, 1=min/max) - DOWN: in settingMode adjusts seaLevelhPa down; otherwise does nothing (or could toggle too) Min/max tracking: Add globals: ```cpp bool statsInit = false; float tMin, tMax, hMin, hMax; ``` Update function after reading: ```cpp void updateMinMax(const Reading &r) { if (!statsInit) { tMin = tMax = r.tempC; hMin = hMax = r.humidity; statsInit = true; return; } if (r.tempC < tMin) tMin = r.tempC; if (r.tempC > tMax) tMax = r.tempC; if (r.humidity < hMin) hMin = r.humidity; if (r.humidity > hMax) hMax = r.humidity; } ``` Screen handling: ```cpp int screenIndex = 0; // 0 main, 1 stats ``` Modify button handler: on short UP press in normal mode, toggle screen: Inside UP release handling: ```cpp if (dt > 800) { settingMode = !settingMode; if (!settingMode) saveSeaLevel(); // save when exiting setting } else { if (settingMode) { seaLevelhPa += 0.5; } else { screenIndex = (screenIndex + 1) % 2; } } ``` Display functions: Main screen (as before, plus altitude and filename maybe). Stats screen: ```cpp void showStatsScreen(const Reading &r) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0, 0); display.print("Min/Max since start"); display.setCursor(0, 14); display.print("Tmin: "); display.print(tMin, 1); display.println(" C"); display.setCursor(0, 24); display.print("Tmax: "); display.print(tMax, 1); display.println(" C"); display.setCursor(0, 38); display.print("Hmin: "); display.print(hMin, 1); display.println(" %"); display.setCursor(0, 48); display.print("Hmax: "); display.print(hMax, 1); display.println(" %"); display.display(); } ``` And in your periodic update: ```cpp Reading r = readAll(); updateMinMax(r); if (settingMode) { // show setting screen } else if (screenIndex == 0) { showReading(r); } else { showStatsScreen(r); } ``` You might also want a “reset stats” function (like long press DOWN) to clear min/max. Do you want the stats to persist across reset (EEPROM), or just “since boot” as you said?
Since boot is fine. One more thing: sometimes the OLED flickers when updating. Can I reduce flicker?

Flicker usually comes from clearing the whole display and redrawing frequently. With SSD1306 libraries, `display.display()` pushes the whole buffer over I2C, so doing it too often can look like flicker. Ways to reduce it: 1) Update less often If you update every 2 seconds, flicker should be minimal. If you’re updating multiple times per second, slow it down. 2) Avoid unnecessary redraws Only redraw when values change meaningfully or when the screen changes. 3) Don’t call `display.clearDisplay()` every time Instead, overwrite only the regions that change. But with Adafruit_SSD1306, you still send the full buffer, so the main benefit is visual (you avoid blanking between frames). You can: - draw a filled black rectangle over the text area you want to refresh (`fillRect`) and redraw text there, leaving the rest intact. Example: ```cpp display.fillRect(0, 24, 128, 40, SSD1306_BLACK); // clear lower area only ``` 4) Use a “double-buffered feel” by drawing everything before calling display.display() You’re already doing that (clear -> draw -> display). The flicker is often the visible blanking from clearDisplay. If you replace clearDisplay with selective fillRect, the header stays stable. 5) Increase I2C speed Faster buffer transfer can reduce visible update artifacts: ```cpp Wire.setClock(400000); // 400kHz fast mode ``` Put it after `Wire.begin();` in setup. Most SSD1306 modules and RTC/BME/BH1750 handle 400kHz fine. If something becomes unreliable, drop back to 100kHz. A good combo: - `Wire.setClock(400000);` - Replace `clearDisplay()` with targeted `fillRect` clearing - Update at 1 Hz or slower If you tell me your current update interval and what parts of the screen change, I can suggest a specific layout and which rectangles to clear.

Update interval is 1 second. I clear the whole display each time. I like having the timestamp at top and values below. Could you show a version that keeps the header stable and only clears the value area?

Sure. Idea: draw a static header line (date/time) and maybe file name, then only clear/redraw the area where sensor values are printed. Example main screen: - Line 0: date - Line 1: time - Lines below: values Implementation: ```cpp void showMainScreen(const Reading &r) { // Update header area (top 16 px) without clearing it first (or clear just header) display.fillRect(0, 0, 128, 16, SSD1306_BLACK); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0, 0); display.print(r.now.year()); display.print("-"); if (r.now.month() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.month()); display.print("-"); if (r.now.day() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.day()); display.setCursor(0, 8); if (r.now.hour() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.hour()); display.print(":"); if (r.now.minute() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.minute()); display.print(":"); if (r.now.second() < 10) display.print("0"); display.print(r.now.second()); // Clear value area only (from y=16 to bottom) display.fillRect(0, 16, 128, 48, SSD1306_BLACK); display.setCursor(0, 18); display.print("T: "); display.print(r.tempC, 1); display.println(" C"); display.setCursor(0, 28); display.print("H: "); display.print(r.humidity, 1); display.println(" %"); display.setCursor(0, 38); display.print("P: "); display.print(r.pressure_hPa, 1); display.println(" hPa"); display.setCursor(0, 48); display.print("Alt: "); display.print(r.altitude_m, 0); display.println(" m"); display.setCursor(0, 58); display.print("L: "); display.print(r.lux, 0); display.print(" lx"); display.display(); } ``` This avoids a full-screen clear and tends to look steadier. Also consider setting I2C speed: ```cpp Wire.begin(); Wire.setClock(400000); ``` For the stats screen, you can do the same: clear only the area you change. If you still see flicker at 1 Hz, it could be the OLED power supply dipping or I2C noise, but usually the partial-clear technique helps. Want to also add a small indicator like “S” when in settingMode, without redrawing everything?

Yes, add a small “SET” indicator in the corner when in setting mode, and show seaLevelhPa on that screen. Also I’m curious: what’s the difference between BME280 and BMP280? Did I pick the right sensor?

For the “SET” indicator and a setting screen: Approach: - If `settingMode` is true, show a dedicated screen: - Top: “SET SLP” - Show current seaLevelhPa and maybe current pressure - Show computed altitude too so you can see effect Example: ```cpp void showSettingScreen(const Reading &r) { display.fillRect(0, 0, 128, 64, SSD1306_BLACK); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0, 0); display.print("SET SLP (hPa)"); display.setCursor(0, 14); display.print("SLP: "); display.print(seaLevelhPa, 1); display.setCursor(0, 28); display.print("P: "); display.print(r.pressure_hPa, 1); display.setCursor(0, 42); display.print("Alt: "); display.print(r.altitude_m, 0); display.print(" m"); display.setCursor(0, 56); display.print("UP/DN adj, long=exit"); display.display(); } ``` If you want a small “SET” indicator on the main screen when settingMode is true (instead of a dedicated screen), you can draw it at top-right: ```cpp void drawSetIndicator() { display.fillRect(100, 0, 28, 8, SSD1306_BLACK); display.setCursor(100, 0); display.print("SET"); } ``` Call it before `display.display()` when `settingMode` is true. BME280 vs BMP280: - BMP280 measures: temperature + pressure - BME280 measures: temperature + pressure + humidity So for a weather station that includes humidity, BME280 is the right choice. Other practical differences: - BME280 has an extra humidity sensor element and calibration, slightly higher cost. - Both have similar pressure/temperature performance for hobby use. - Humidity readings on BME280 are decent but can be affected by heat from nearby components; keep it away from warm parts if possible. Since you want humidity, you picked the right one. If you want, I can suggest sensor placement tips (like not putting it right next to the Uno’s voltage regulator) and basic averaging to smooth noise.
Good to know. About smoothing: readings jump a bit. Can you implement a moving average for temperature and humidity (say over last 10 samples) but keep pressure and lux raw?

Yes. A simple moving average buffer is straightforward. We’ll keep circular buffers for temp and humidity, size N=10: - store last N values - maintain running sum for efficiency Globals: ```cpp const int AVG_N = 10; float tBuf[AVG_N]; float hBuf[AVG_N]; int avgIndex = 0; int avgCount = 0; float tSum = 0; float hSum = 0; float tAvg = NAN; float hAvg = NAN; ``` Update function called each time you read new raw values: ```cpp void updateAverages(float t, float h) { if (avgCount < AVG_N) { // buffer not full yet tBuf[avgIndex] = t; hBuf[avgIndex] = h; tSum += t; hSum += h; avgCount++; avgIndex = (avgIndex + 1) % AVG_N; } else { // subtract oldest, add newest tSum -= tBuf[avgIndex]; hSum -= hBuf[avgIndex]; tBuf[avgIndex] = t; hBuf[avgIndex] = h; tSum += t; hSum += h; avgIndex = (avgIndex + 1) % AVG_N; } tAvg = tSum / avgCount; hAvg = hSum / avgCount; } ``` Then in `readAll()`: - read raw temp/humidity - call updateAverages(rawTemp, rawHum) - store averaged values in Reading (or store both raw and avg) Simplest: store averaged values as tempC/humidity: ```cpp Reading readAll() { Reading r; r.now = rtc.now(); float tRaw = bme.readTemperature(); float hRaw = bme.readHumidity(); updateAverages(tRaw, hRaw); r.tempC = tAvg; r.humidity = hAvg; r.pressure_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; r.seaLevel_hPa = seaLevelhPa; r.altitude_m = bme.readAltitude(seaLevelhPa); r.lux = lightMeter.readLightLevel(); return r; } ``` Note: altitude uses pressure and sea level; if temp/humidity are averaged but pressure is raw, that’s okay.
