Thanks. I’m going to order a flux pen, wick, and maybe 63/37. For now I’ll continue with the chisel tip and be careful. If I come back with issues after powering up, can we troubleshoot?

Yes. When you get to power-up, the most useful info will be: - Supply voltage at the input - 12V rail measurement on the board - 5V regulator output - Any unexpected heat - Current draw if you can measure it - Symptoms (no sound, distortion, hum, one channel dead) And if it’s an audio amp, also check grounding and input wiring. For now, keep your tip tinned, use minimal solder, and aim for consistent technique. Once you have flux and wick, rework becomes much less stressful and your joints will improve quickly.

I’m back. I finished soldering everything and did resistance checks. 12V to GND is around 1.2k ohms. 5V to GND is around 300 ohms and slowly rises. Seems okay. I powered it with a 12V adapter and the 7805 output is 5.02V. But I get a loud hum in the speaker output even with no input.

Those resistance readings and regulator output sound reasonable. Hum with no input can come from a few places: grounding issues, input floating, power supply ripple, or a soldering/assembly error around the signal path. Let’s narrow it down: 1) Is the hum 50/60 Hz “mains” hum, or a higher buzz? 2) Does the hum change with volume control position (if there is one)? 3) Are you using a switching wall adapter? Some cheap ones are noisy. 4) Are the input pins/jack properly referenced to ground, or is the input floating when nothing is plugged in? Also, confirm the op-amp is inserted correctly (orientation) and that the electrolytics around power supply and output coupling are oriented right. Can you describe the kit topology—does it use a power amp IC (like LM386, TDA series), or is it discrete?
It uses an LM386 actually. There’s a volume pot on the board. Hum changes with volume: it gets louder when volume is up.

If hum scales with the volume control, that suggests the hum is entering before the volume pot or at the input stage, rather than from the power output stage alone. Common causes: - Input jack ground not connected well (cold joint on jack ground pin) - Input signal line picking up noise because it’s floating when unplugged (some designs need a pulldown resistor) - Bad solder joint on the volume pot ground lug or wiper - Poor decoupling on the LM386 supply pin (missing/incorrect capacitor) Checks: 1) Verify the input jack pins - Many 3.5mm jacks have multiple pins (tip, ring, sleeve, and switch contacts). It’s easy to misinterpret which is ground. - Use your multimeter continuity to identify which jack pin connects to the sleeve (ground) of a plugged-in connector. Then ensure that pin is soldered to board ground. 2) Volume pot wiring - Measure continuity from pot ground pin to board ground. - Confirm the pot wiper goes to the LM386 input as intended. 3) LM386 decoupling - There should be a capacitor from pin 6 (Vcc) to ground close to the IC, often 100 nF plus maybe 47–220 µF electrolytic. - Also check the bypass pin (pin 7) capacitor if used (often 10 µF). Wrong polarity there can cause noise. 4) Input biasing - Many LM386 circuits have an input resistor to ground (like 10k–100k) to prevent floating input. Confirm it’s present and soldered. Quick experiment: - With power on and volume up, short the input to ground (briefly) at the input jack (tip to sleeve) using a clip lead or a screwdriver tip. If hum drops dramatically, the issue is input floating or grounding at input. Can you try that and report what happens?
I shorted the input tip to sleeve at the jack and the hum almost disappears. So it’s probably input floating?

That result strongly indicates the amplifier input is picking up noise when it’s not referenced to ground. Some kits rely on the source device to provide that reference, but it’s better design to include an input pulldown resistor. Look for an input resistor from the LM386 input (pin 3 typically) to ground, often 10k–100k. If it exists, it might be: - Missing (wrong part placement) - Wrong value (too high) - Bad solder joint - Connected to the wrong node due to assembly error Steps: 1) Find the resistor that should go from input to ground (check the kit schematic/BOM). 2) Measure its resistance in-circuit if possible. Or at least check continuity from input node to one end of that resistor, and from the other end to ground. 3) Reflow its joints (preferably with flux once you have it, but you can do it with a tiny bit of fresh solder). If the kit truly doesn’t include a pulldown, you can add one: - Solder a 47k resistor from input signal to ground at the jack or at the pot input. This will keep it quiet with nothing plugged in. Since you said hum changes with volume, another place is the pot: if the pot input is floating when nothing is plugged in, a pulldown at the pot input fixes it. Do you have the kit schematic, or can you identify whether there’s already a resistor from the input line to ground?
There is a 100k resistor near the input jack. I think it’s supposed to go to ground. I checked continuity and one side goes to the input, the other side doesn’t beep to ground.

Then that’s likely the culprit: the “ground” end of that 100k isn’t actually connected to ground—either a bad joint, wrong pad, broken trace, or you placed the resistor in the wrong location. What to do: 1) Inspect the resistor placement - Confirm it’s in the correct footprint per silkscreen and instructions. - Make sure it’s not supposed to connect to a different node (sometimes it goes to bias reference). 2) Reflow both joints - Add a tiny bit of fresh solder to each end and ensure it properly wets the pad and lead. 3) Trace the “ground” end pad - Use continuity to see where that pad goes. Maybe it connects to ground through a via that isn’t soldered well elsewhere. 4) Check the input jack ground pin joint - If the board “ground” near the jack isn’t actually connected to the main ground (due to a broken ground trace or missed solder), you’ll see weird behavior. Quick fix test: - Temporarily connect the “ground” end of that 100k resistor to a known ground point (like DC jack ground) with a clip lead. If hum disappears, you’ve confirmed the missing ground reference. Can you try clipping that resistor end to a known ground and see if it fixes the hum?
I clipped it to the DC jack ground and the hum is gone even with nothing plugged in. So the resistor wasn’t grounded. I reflowed it and now it beeps to ground. Hum is gone.

Nice—textbook case. That was effectively a missing input pulldown due to a bad ground connection at that resistor pad. This also reinforces why cold joints matter: a joint can look “okay-ish” but be electrically open or high resistance. Once your flux pen arrives, you can do a touch-up pass on other suspicious joints: - Any ground connections - The volume pot pins - The LM386 pins - Power input connectors But if it’s working now, avoid unnecessary rework. Any remaining issues—distortion, low volume, oscillation, or noise when touching the input?
It sounds good now. But when I touch the input jack metal, I hear a little buzz. Is that normal?

Some buzz when touching the input or jack can be normal because your body couples noise into the high-impedance input. It should be mild, and it should go away or reduce when an input source is connected. To minimize it: - Ensure the jack sleeve is solidly grounded. - Keep input traces short (already fixed by PCB). - Make sure the input pulldown is present (now it is). - Ensure the enclosure/shielding (if any) is properly grounded—though if you’re not using one, some sensitivity is expected. If the buzz is loud, it could mean the input is still high impedance or the ground is not solid. But a small buzz on touch is pretty typical for audio amps. Since your soldering is now producing functional results, the next step is refining technique and making joints consistent. Once you get flux and wick, you’ll find rework and cleanup much easier.
Flux pen and wick arrived. Any tips on using them without making a mess?

Flux pen: - Apply sparingly: a quick swipe on the joint is enough. - Reflow: touch iron to joint, add a tiny amount of solder if needed, remove. - Clean if desired with IPA after. Wick: 1) Put a little flux on the wick end (even if it’s “fluxed wick,” extra helps). 2) Place wick on the solder you want to remove. 3) Place iron on top of the wick; wait until solder flows into it. 4) Lift iron and wick together (don’t drag). 5) Cut off the used wick section; don’t reuse a saturated part. To avoid mess: - Don’t over-flux; it will spread and get sticky. - Don’t leave the wick heated too long; it can overheat pads. - After using flux/wick, clean the area with IPA so you can inspect. Great use cases: - Fixing small bridges - Removing excess blobs - Cleaning up a joint that has too much solder - Clearing holes during rework If you want, tell me one joint on your board you’re not satisfied with visually, and I’ll suggest a targeted touch-up method.
The DC jack joints are huge and ugly. They work, but I want them cleaner.

For big connector joints, “clean” often means “smooth and properly filleted,” but they’ll naturally be larger than resistor joints. Here’s a cleanup approach: 1) Add flux around the joint. 2) If there’s clearly too much solder, use wick: - Place wick on the blob and heat until it absorbs some solder. - Don’t try to remove all solder—just reduce it. 3) Reflow to shape: - Add a tiny bit of fresh solder (optional) and reheat the joint so it forms a smooth fillet around the pin. - Remove iron and let it cool undisturbed. Goal: - You want a fillet that covers the pad and pin, not a giant dome. - Ensure it still fully fills the hole; for mechanical strength, through-hole connectors benefit from a solid fill. Afterward, clean with IPA so you can judge the shape. If you find the joint refuses to smooth, it might be because the joint is cooling too fast; keep heat on slightly longer so it fully liquefies before you remove the iron.
I tried wick and now I’m worried I removed too much. The joint looks smaller and I can see a bit of the hole edge.

Seeing the hole edge isn’t automatically bad. The key is whether the pin is properly bonded and the plated-through hole is filled enough for mechanical strength. Check: - Does the solder still wet the pin all around? - Is there a visible fillet at the pin base? - If you look from the other side, do you see solder there too (if it wicked through)? - Mechanical: gently wiggle the jack; the pins shouldn’t move relative to the pad. If you think it’s underfilled: - Add flux. - Heat the joint and feed a small amount of solder until you see it flow and form a proper fillet. Stop there. For connector pins, slightly more solder is acceptable. “Pretty” isn’t as important as “solid.” If the DC jack is subject to mechanical stress, consider also that the jack body may have mechanical tabs; ensure those are soldered well too.
Makes sense. I think it’s solid. I’m feeling more confident now. Any final advice to avoid cold joints in the future?

Core habits that prevent cold joints: - Use a chisel tip and keep it tinned and clean. - Use flux proactively, especially for lead-free, rework, and ground planes. - Heat pad and lead together; feed solder to the joint, not the tip. - Don’t move the joint while cooling. - Don’t keep re-heating repeatedly; if it’s not working, stop and fix the root cause (tip oxidation, lack of flux, insufficient contact). - Use appropriate solder diameter for the work. - Inspect and test as you go; catch issues early. With the flux pen and wick, you’ve got the main tools that separate “frustrating” from “repeatable.” If you start another project, you’ll likely notice your joints improve rapidly just from muscle memory and better materials.
I can't decide between Cherry MX Brown, Gateron Yellow, and Holy Pandas. What's the actual feel difference?

Cherry MX Brown, Gateron Yellow, and Holy Pandas sit in three different “feel” buckets even though people often cross-shop them. 1) Cherry MX Brown (tactile, light, mild bump) - Feel: A small, rounded tactile bump early in the keypress, then a relatively smooth descent. Many describe it as “barely tactile” compared to enthusiast tactiles. - Sound: Medium, somewhat higher-pitched “clack” depending on board and keycaps; not inherently loud. - Weight: Typically ~45cN actuation with a light tactile event; bottom-out around the mid-50s to ~60cN. - Use case: If you want a hint of feedback without heavy tactility, and you want predictable, mainstream behavior. 2) Gateron Yellow (linear, medium, smooth for the price) - Feel: No bump—just a straight press. Notably smooth in many batches, especially compared to stock Cherry Browns. People like them because they’re “buttery” once lubed and not too heavy. - Sound: Deeper than many Cherry switches, especially in a board with a lower resonance; still depends heavily on case and plate. - Weight: Often ~50cN actuation and ~60–65cN bottom-out (varies by variant: KS-3, milky top, etc.). - Use case: If you want consistent keypresses, good for gaming and fast typing if you like linears. 3) Holy Panda (tactile, strong bump, “poppy”) - Feel: A large, crisp tactile bump that dominates the keypress. Often described as “punchy” or “snappy.” The tactility is much more pronounced than Browns. - Sound: Characteristic “thock/pop” when tuned well; can be loud because the bump encourages bottoming out, and the switch itself has a distinct acoustic profile. - Weight: Commonly ~67g bottom-out (again depends on springs), with a strong tactile event that can feel heavy even if the spring isn’t extreme. - Use case: If you want obvious tactility and a more “eventful” keystroke. If you describe what you want in terms of: (a) how much tactile feedback, (b) how heavy you like keys, (c) whether you want a deeper or higher-pitched sound, it gets easier to pick. What’s the target: smooth and quiet, or more “character” and feedback?

I’m aiming for a really satisfying tactile typing feel, but I don’t want fatigue. I type a lot, but I also play games. I keep hearing Browns are “scratchy,” Yellows are “budget king,” and Holy Pandas are “overhyped.” How do those stereotypes map to reality?

The stereotypes map to a few real traits, but they get exaggerated because people compare very different things. “Browns are scratchy” - Reality: Stock Cherry Browns often feel scratchier than many modern Gateron/JWK linears, especially if you’re sensitive to stem/leaf friction. The scratch isn’t always severe; it’s just more noticeable because the tactile leaf contact plus Cherry tooling can produce a dry, slightly raspy travel. - Important nuance: Scratchiness varies by batch, and it also depends on keycap profile and plate material because those change how vibration is perceived. Lube can help a lot, but with Browns you have to be careful not to lube the tactile legs/leaf interface too much or you’ll erase the already-small bump. “Yellows are budget king” - Reality: Gateron Yellows are widely liked because they’re inexpensive, reasonably smooth stock, and respond very well to lube/films. They also sit in a very usable weight range. The “king” part is mostly value-per-dollar, not that they’re objectively the best linear. - Important nuance: There are multiple Yellow variants (KS-3, KS-9, milky top, full milky). Sound and smoothness differ. In some boards, a milky housing makes them deeper. “Holy Pandas are overhyped” - Reality: Holy Pandas are legitimately a strong tactile with a distinctive sound/feel. The hype comes from (1) the history (original frankenswitch), (2) the big tactile event being dramatic compared to mainstream switches, and (3) the sound signature in popular builds. - Why people bounce off them: The bump is large and early, and many users end up bottoming out harder. That can cause fatigue if you type long sessions and tend to “slam” keys. Also, not all Holy Panda implementations are equal—different manufacturers, different tolerances, inconsistent lubing, etc. If you tried a mediocre batch in a harsh board, it’s easy to call them overhyped. Given your goals (tactile satisfaction, low fatigue, mixed typing/gaming), the key variable is tactile shape: - Browns: small bump, low risk of fatigue, but might feel boring. - Holy Panda: big bump, high satisfaction, potentially fatiguing, and can feel “sticky” if you don’t like strong tactility. - There are also “middle ground” tactiles (e.g., Boba U4T, Durock T1, Zealio V2, Kailh Box Brown/Box Silent Brown) that often nail “satisfying tactile” without being as polarizing as HP. Do you have any reference point for preferred spring weight? For example, have you used 45g linears, 62g linears, or heavier tactiles before?

No firm reference. I’ve only used cheap prebuilts with generic “brown” clones and some laptop keyboards. I’m building a 75% with a PC plate and gasket mount. I want it to sound deeper, not clacky. I’m thinking PBT keycaps, maybe Cherry profile. How does that steer the switch choice?

PC plate + gasket mount + PBT Cherry profile already pushes you toward a deeper, less metallic sound than, say, aluminum plate + tray mount. That’s good for “thockier” builds, but the switch still matters. How your build choices interact with switches: - PC plate: Softer, tends to damp higher frequencies and reduce sharpness. It can also add a bit of flex, which can make tactiles feel slightly “rounder” and linears feel a touch softer on bottom-out. - Gasket mount: Usually reduces case ping and can soften bottom-out. It often emphasizes a lower, more muted signature if the case has good internal damping. - PBT Cherry profile: PBT often yields a slightly less sharp top-out than ABS, and Cherry profile can sound deeper than taller profiles like SA (though SA can be deep too, just different). Cherry profile also changes leverage and typing angle, which can subtly change perceived force. Switch implications: - Cherry Browns: In a deep-sounding board, Browns can still sound fairly “clacky” because their bump is mild and you may bottom out with more speed. Also Cherry housings can have a brighter top-out unless damped. - Gateron Yellows: In your setup, Yellows can become very “creamy” and deep if lubed and paired with the right housing variant. But they’re linear; the “satisfying tactile feel” goal wouldn’t be met unless you decide you actually like linears. - Holy Pandas: PC plate + gasket can tame some harshness and make the sound quite pleasing. But the big bump can still encourage bottoming out. With PBT caps and a damped case, you can get a deep “pop/thock,” but it might be loud. Given you want tactile but not fatiguing, I’d seriously consider tactiles that have: - A defined bump but not a brick-wall feel, - A medium spring (e.g., 62–67g bottom-out), - Good housing acoustics (often nylon bottom, POM stem, etc.). Examples in that direction: - Gazzew Boba U4T (thocky tactile, strong but rounded bump; many love them in gasket builds). - Durock T1 / Koala (tactile with a noticeable bump; can be tuned to be less harsh). - TTC Bluish White (snappy tactile, often less “muddy” but still satisfying). - Kailh Box switches (Box Brown is mild; Box Royal is very tactile but can be sharp). If you want to stay strictly among your three options: for your board goals, Holy Panda is the only one that delivers “satisfying tactile” in the obvious way. But you’d want to tune it (lube carefully, maybe spring swap) to avoid fatigue. Do you want the bump to be early (right at the top) or more centered (mid-press)? Early bumps feel more “clicky without click,” centered bumps can feel more controlled.

Early bump is fine. I just don’t want it to feel like I’m pushing over a curb every keystroke. Also I keep seeing people talk about “tactile event” and “force curve.” Can you explain those in practical terms, like what my fingers actually feel?

“Tactile event” and “force curve” are basically ways to describe the shape of resistance your finger experiences as the switch moves. Force curve (practical view) Imagine plotting how hard you’re pressing (force) vs. how far the key has moved (travel). Different switches trace different shapes: - Linear: Force rises smoothly as you press down (mostly spring force). Your finger feels consistent resistance; there’s no sudden change. - Tactile: Force rises, then suddenly drops (or drops sharply) at the bump, then rises again toward bottom-out. That sudden change is the tactile event. Tactile event (what your finger feels) The tactile event is the “moment” you feel the bump: the switch resists you, then it gives way. That “give” is the tactile sensation. Key practical descriptors: 1) Bump size (magnitude) - Small bump: You feel a subtle “nudge.” That’s Cherry Brown territory. - Large bump: You feel a clear “pop” or “step.” That’s Holy Panda territory. 2) Bump shape (sharp vs rounded) - Sharp bump: Feels crisp, almost like a snap. Some switches feel like a sudden ledge and then a drop. - Rounded bump: Feels more like rolling over a hill. It can be satisfying without being jarring. 3) Bump position (early vs mid) - Early bump: You feel it almost immediately. This can help prevent bottoming out if you learn to stop after actuation, but many people still bottom out. - Mid bump: The first part of travel is light, then you hit the bump. Some prefer this because it feels less abrupt at the top. 4) Post-bump behavior - Big drop after bump: Can feel “poppy” but also can feel unstable if the force collapses too much. - Small drop: Feels more controlled but less dramatic. How this maps to your “curb” concern When people say “pushing over a curb,” they usually mean: - A sharp, high bump very early, plus - A large force drop right after the bump, so your finger feels like it’s stuck, then suddenly falls through. Holy Pandas can feel like that to some people because the tactile event is big and early. Browns usually won’t; they’re too mild. There are tactiles with strong but smoother bumps (more “hill” than “curb”)—that’s where many modern enthusiast tactiles shine. If you want an early bump but not curb-like, you’d likely prefer a tactile with: - A rounded bump profile, - Moderate peak force (not too high), - Not too dramatic a force drop. Do you plan to lube and/or film switches? That tuning can change perceived sharpness and sound a lot.
Yes, I’m willing to lube. Not sure about filming. Also, is lubing tactiles risky? I don’t want to ruin the bump. What lube and where exactly would you apply it for these switches?

Lubing tactiles is a little more “surgical” than lubing linears, but it’s not inherently risky if you avoid the tactile contact surfaces. General rules for tactiles - Goal: Reduce scratch and spring noise while preserving the tactile event. - Don’t lube: The tactile legs (the part of the stem that interacts with the leaf) and the leaf contact area inside the housing—unless you intentionally want to reduce tactility. Lubing Cherry MX Brown (tactile, mild bump) - Risk: High risk of turning them into “almost linear” because the bump is already small. - Recommended lube: A thinner lube like Krytox 205g0 can work if applied extremely lightly; some prefer Tribosys 3203 (thinner than 3204) for tactiles. - Where: - Stem rails (the long sliding sides): very light coat. - Stem pole (bottom): optional, a tiny amount can deepen sound but can also increase mush if overdone. - Housing rails: light. - Spring: bag lube with Krytox 105 oil or donut dip with 205g0; or use GPL 105. - Avoid: - Tactile legs (the bump faces) and the leaf area. Lubing Holy Pandas (strong tactile) - Risk: Lower risk of “losing the bump” because the bump is big; you have more margin. But over-lubing can still dull the crispness. - Recommended lube: Tribosys 3203 or 3204 are common for tactiles. 3203 keeps it a bit crisper; 3204/205g0 can make it smoother and deeper but may soften the bump. - Where: - Stem rails: moderate-light coat. - Stem legs: either leave completely dry, or do a very light “edge” lube away from the contact point if you know what you’re doing. Most people skip legs entirely. - Housing rails: light. - Springs: same as above. - Notes: - Holy Panda housings/stems vary by maker; some have more leaf noise. Lubing can reduce leaf ping, but don’t paint the leaf. Lubing Gateron Yellow (linear) - Risk: Minimal; linears are forgiving. - Recommended lube: Krytox 205g0 is the classic. 205 on stem + housing and 105 on springs yields a very smooth linear. - Where: - Stem rails: normal coat. - Stem pole: optional; can deepen sound but if you use too much, you can get “lube stick” or sluggish return. - Housing rails: normal coat. - Springs: 105 oil or bag lube. - Filming: - Many Gateron Yellows benefit from films if housing wobble is noticeable and you want a tighter sound. Not always necessary. Filming (when it matters) Films reduce housing wobble and can change acoustics (often deeper/tighter). They matter more when: - The top and bottom housings have loose tolerances. - You’re chasing consistency and a more solid sound. If you’re building a gasket 75% with PC plate, filming can be worthwhile for linears; for tactiles it depends on the switch. If you tell me which Holy Panda version you’re considering (Drop, Glorious, YOK-derived, etc.) and which Yellow variant (KS-3, milky, etc.), I can be more specific.

I was looking at Drop Holy Pandas, and for Yellows probably the milky top/black bottom ones. For Browns, just standard Cherry. Also, can you describe the sound differences in words? Like “thock,” “clack,” “pop”—I get lost.

Drop Holy Pandas + milky-top Yellows + stock Cherry Browns is a pretty classic comparison set. Sound vocabulary (what people usually mean) - Clack: Higher-frequency, sharper transient. Often comes from harder materials (aluminum plate, thin ABS caps), higher-pitched switch top-out, or less internal damping. “Clack” is not necessarily bad; it’s just brighter. - Thock: Lower-frequency, more muted, “round” sound. Often associated with softer plates (PC), thicker caps, damped cases, and switches with deeper housing resonance. - Pop: A short, percussive, slightly higher “thock” with a crisp attack—often used for strong tactiles like Holy Pandas where the tactile event and bottom-out produce a distinctive per-key “pock/pop.” - Creamy: A smooth, lower-mid sound with less high-frequency noise; often used for well-lubed linears with good damping. - Ping: Metallic ringing resonance, usually from springs, plate, or case. Lubing springs and adding case/plate damping helps. Now your specific switches: Cherry MX Brown (stock) - Sound tendency: More “clack” than “thock” in many builds, partly because Cherry housings can have a brighter top-out. On PC plate and gasket mount, you can tame it, but Browns often still sound relatively crisp and slightly “plasticky” compared to deeper nylon housings. - With lube: You can reduce scratch and soften the top-out, but if you overdo it you lose the already mild tactile feedback. Gateron Yellow (milky top, black bottom) - Sound tendency: Often “deeper/creamier” than clear-top variants. The milky top can soften the top-out pitch; the black nylon bottom can add depth. - With lube: They can get very “creamy” and low in pitch. They won’t do “pop” because they’re linear, but they can do a satisfying “thock” if the board and caps support it. Drop Holy Panda - Sound tendency: “Pop/thock” with a pronounced per-keystroke character. They can be loud because the tactile bump often leads to decisive bottom-outs. - With lube: The sound can become deeper and less scratchy; the “pop” can become more “thock.” Too much lube can make them sound duller and feel less crisp. If you want a deeper sound but also tactile satisfaction, Drop Holy Pandas can deliver that in your build, but consider spring weight and lubrication to avoid fatigue. Another trick is to use slightly softer landing via plate foam or switch pads, but that changes feel. What’s your tolerance for volume? Holy Pandas can be “fun loud” unless you specifically aim for a quieter build.

I don’t need it silent, but I don’t want it to be obnoxious in a normal room. I’m also confused about actuation vs bottom-out. People say “don’t bottom out” to avoid fatigue, but I always bottom out on laptop keyboards. Is it realistic not to bottom out on a mechanical?

For most people, especially coming from laptops, it’s not realistic to never bottom out on a mechanical—at least not at first. The more useful goal is “reduce harsh bottom-outs” and “avoid slamming,” rather than “never bottom out.” Actuation vs bottom-out - Actuation point: Where the switch actually registers a keypress (electrically). On many MX-style switches this is around ~2.0 mm, but it varies. - Bottom-out: When the key hits the end of travel (around ~4.0 mm in many MX switches). Why bottoming out happens - Habit: Laptop keyboards have short travel and a hard bottom. You learn to press until you hit the end to be sure it registered. - Feedback: If the tactile event isn’t distinct, you may keep pressing until bottom-out to confirm. - Speed: When typing fast, your fingers carry momentum; even if you “know” actuation happened, your finger may still finish the stroke. Can you learn not to bottom out? - With strong tactiles, some people learn to ride the bump and stop early, because the bump provides a clear cue. But strong tactiles can also cause you to bottom out harder if you push past the bump aggressively. - With linears, it’s harder because there’s no tactile cue; you often bottom out unless you consciously train, and even then it’s inconsistent. Fatigue is more about force and impact than bottoming out itself - A heavy spring can tire muscles. - A harsh bottom-out (hard plate, no damping) can tire joints. - A big tactile bump can tire you if you’re fighting it repeatedly. Given your build (PC plate + gasket), bottom-out should be less harsh than many setups. You can also tune: - Spring weight (lighter reduces required force, but can increase accidental presses). - Keycap profile (changes leverage and sound). - O-rings (generally not recommended for feel; they can make bottom-out mushy). - Plate foam / case foam (sound and some feel change). - Switch choice: a tactile with a rounded bump can reduce the “slam” feeling. If you want “satisfying tactile but not obnoxious,” I’d lean toward a tactile that’s not as explosively poppy as Holy Panda, or you’d need to tune it carefully. Do you want a “clean, crisp tactile” (more definition) or a “rounded, cushioned tactile” (more comfort)?
Probably rounded, cushioned tactile. But I still want it to feel premium, not mushy. How do U4T compare to Holy Pandas in that sense? And would those be better than Browns?

U4T vs Holy Panda is a very common fork for exactly your preferences. Boba U4T (thocky tactile) - Feel: Strong tactile, generally rounded rather than razor-sharp. Many people describe it as “big bump but not harsh.” The bump is prominent early, but it tends to feel more like a firm hill than a sharp curb. - Sound: Deep “thock,” often less “poppy” and less high-frequency than Holy Pandas. Still not quiet—U4T is the “thock” version; U4 (no T) is the silent tactile. - Premium feel: Very good housing tolerances; often less wobble. People like the solidity. - Fatigue: Can still be fatiguing if you go heavy (e.g., 68g) and type hard, but many find it more comfortable than Holy Pandas because the tactile event is less abrupt. Holy Panda - Feel: Strong tactile, often sharper and more “snappy.” The bump can feel like a distinct step. Very “characterful.” - Sound: More “pop” and upper-mid presence. Can be louder/more attention-grabbing. - Premium feel: Depends on batch; Drop HPs are generally decent, but the feel can be more polarizing. Cherry Browns - Feel: Mild tactile. If you want “rounded cushioned tactile,” Browns can feel like “barely there,” which is why people graduate away from them. - Sound: Often more clack than thock, unless everything else in the build is tuned deep. In your case: - If you want a premium tactile experience and you’re already considering Holy Pandas, U4T is very likely to satisfy you with less risk of “obnoxious pop” and less curb-like feel. - Browns would be the safe, mild option but probably won’t hit the “satisfying” target. If you want, you can also tune U4T with spring swaps (e.g., 62g or 65g) to reduce fatigue while keeping tactility. Do you have a preference for return force? Some people dislike slow return on heavier tactiles for gaming, especially on repeated key taps.
Yeah, I don’t want sluggish return. For gaming I need quick repeats. Would a strong tactile slow me down compared to Yellows? Also, what about long pole stems? I keep seeing that term.

Strong tactiles can feel slower for rapid repeats compared to linears, but it’s not purely speed—it's about how your fingers interact with the force curve. Gaming and strong tactiles - Rapid tapping: Linears often feel easiest because there’s no bump resisting repeated actuations. You can “flutter” the key with minimal interruption. - Strong tactiles: The bump adds a resistance point you cross each time. That can feel slower or more fatiguing for repeated presses (think strafing keys) if you’re sensitive to it. - However: Many people game fine on strong tactiles. The difference is more about preference and the specific game (fast movement vs slower, deliberate inputs). Return force and spring choice - Heavier springs can increase return force (snappier upstroke), which helps repeated taps—but also increases downstroke force and potential fatigue. - Lighter springs reduce fatigue but can feel less “snappy” on return and can cause accidental presses if you rest fingers heavily. Long pole stems (what it means) - In MX switches, the “stem pole” is the central post that hits the bottom housing at bottom-out. - A long pole stem bottoms out on the pole earlier than the normal stem would, effectively shortening travel (e.g., from ~4.0 mm to ~3.5 mm or less). - Effects: 1) Sound: Often produces a sharper, more “clacky” bottom-out (a hard pole impact), though in a damped board it can still be pleasant. 2) Feel: Shorter travel can feel “snappier” and more immediate. Some people like it for gaming because keys reset faster and you don’t have to move as far. 3) Harshness: Can feel harsher if the impact is hard and your board is stiff. Holy Pandas and U4Ts - Some tactile designs have long-pole-like behavior or harder bottom-outs; others are more traditional. - If you want deep and cushioned, you might prefer avoiding very long-pole, ultra-clacky switches unless your build heavily dampens them. If gaming performance is a top concern, a common compromise is: - Use tactiles for alpha keys (typing feel), - Use linears for gaming cluster (WASD, space) — but that’s more complex and can feel inconsistent. Would you consider mixing switches, or do you want uniform feel across the board?

I’d rather keep it uniform. Mixing sounds like a rabbit hole. If I choose U4T, what spring weight would you recommend for not-fatiguing but still stable? And how would that compare to Gateron Yellow in terms of force?

For a uniform board that’s good for typing and still usable for gaming, a medium spring in a strong tactile is usually the sweet spot. U4T spring weight suggestions - 62g bottom-out (or “62g” spring): Often a good balance—less fatigue than 68g, still stable, still returns well. - 65g: Slightly more resistance and snappier return; can be better if you have a heavy resting finger style or want fewer accidental presses. - 68g (stock in some U4T variants): Great for a “solid” feel and strong return, but more fatigue risk for long typing sessions, especially with strong tactility. How this compares to Gateron Yellow - Gateron Yellow is usually around 50g actuation and ~60–65g bottom-out. It’s linear, so the force is “smoothly increasing.” - A U4T with a 62g spring might have a similar bottom-out force to a Yellow, but it will feel heavier because you’re also overcoming the tactile bump. The peak force at the bump can be noticeably higher than a linear’s force at the same travel point. - So: even if bottom-out numbers look similar, tactiles often “feel heavier” in motion. Gaming stability - If you’re worried about accidental presses, tactiles actually help because the bump discourages accidental actuation from light touches. - For repeated taps, the bump is the trade-off. If you can only pick one spring weight without trying: 62g is the conservative “not-fatiguing” choice while staying premium and stable. If you want a bit more snap and don’t mind effort, 65g. Do you plan to use any foam (plate foam/case foam) or a PE sheet? Those can shift sound dramatically, sometimes more than the switch choice.
I was thinking plate foam and maybe a thin case foam, but I’m not sure about PE foam. People say PE makes everything poppy. Can you break down what each foam does and how it interacts with tactiles?

Sure—foam choices are basically acoustic and resonance tuning, with some side effects on feel. Plate foam (between plate and PCB) - Primary effect: Reduces hollowness and high-frequency resonance; can make the sound more “solid” and less airy. - With tactiles: Often makes the bump feel a bit more “contained” because the board is less resonant; can also reduce “tick” noises from stabilizers and plate vibrations. - Downsides: Can reduce some of the natural “open” character; in some builds it can make things sound muted. Case foam (in the bottom of the case) - Primary effect: Reduces case cavity resonance and echo. Helps with “hollow” sound and can reduce ping. - With tactiles: Often a net positive—tactiles can be loud and case foam can tame the overall volume and harshness. - Downsides: If overstuffed, it can make the board sound dull and can affect gasket compression if it interferes with the PCB. PE foam (thin sheet between PCB and switches or between PCB and plate depending on method) - Primary effect: Adds a sharper, more “poppy” high-frequency component to the sound—often called the “PE foam pop.” - With tactiles: Can emphasize the “pop” of a tactile switch, making it more percussive and sometimes louder. If you want deep and cushioned, PE can push you toward a brighter, more snappy sound. - Downsides: It can make many switches sound similar (homogenizes), and some people find it artificial. It can also increase static risk if you use the wrong material (most people use anti-static or safe PE foam, but it’s a consideration). Tape mod (masking tape on the back of PCB) - Primary effect: Can deepen sound and reduce high-frequency resonance. Often paired with foams. - With tactiles: Can make things sound fuller and reduce harshness, but results vary. For your goal (deep, not clacky, rounded tactile, not obnoxious): - Plate foam + modest case foam is a safe baseline. - I’d skip PE foam initially. Build without it, evaluate, then add if you want more “pop” and presence. Also consider stabilizer tuning and keycap thickness—those often dominate perceived quality. A great switch in a board with rattly stabs won’t feel premium. Do you already have stabilizers picked (screw-in vs clip-in) and a plan for tuning (lube, pads)?
Screw-in stabs, probably TX or Durock. I’ll lube them. But I’m not sure how stab tuning affects sound compared to switch choice. Also, for tactiles, do stabs matter more because you press harder?

Stabilizers matter a lot because they control the biggest keys (space, enter, backspace, shift), and those keys produce a disproportionate amount of your board’s perceived sound and “quality.” How stabs affect sound/feel - Rattle: The classic “cheap keyboard” sound. Even if your switches are perfect, rattly stabs ruin the experience. - Tick: A subtle high-pitched click when the wire taps the housing or PCB. Often shows up after some use if tolerances are off or lube migrates. - Mush: Over-dampening or too much lube can make stabilized keys feel sluggish or muted compared to other keys. Relative impact vs switch choice - Switch choice affects every key, but stabilized keys are so noticeable that bad stabs can dominate your impression. - A board with average switches and excellent stabs often feels more premium than a board with premium switches and mediocre stabs. Do tactiles make stabs “more important”? - Indirectly: Strong tactiles can encourage decisive keypresses, which can make any stab issues more audible (rattle/tick). Also, spacebar gets hit a lot and often harder. - But even on linears, stabs matter just as much; it’s just that tactiles can highlight inconsistencies. TX vs Durock - Both can be good; TX often praised for consistency and less ticking, Durock for availability and solid design (though some batches have fit issues). - Tuning basics: - Wire balancing (critical). - Dielectric grease or thicker lube on wire ends/contact points. - A lighter lube on housings/sliders (205g0 is common). - Optional: stab pads or “holee mod” style band-aid strip to reduce tick (with trade-offs). Given you’re chasing deep sound, a well-tuned spacebar is key. Spacebar acoustics also depend on keycap thickness and whether the spacebar is warped. Are you planning a 6.25u or 7u spacebar layout? And do you have a keycap set in mind (thick PBT vs thinner)?
6.25u, standard. Keycaps: thick PBT, probably around 1.5mm. Cherry profile. Also, what about switch films—do U4Ts need them? And do Holy Pandas benefit?

With thick PBT Cherry caps and a 6.25u spacebar, you’re already set up for a deeper baseline sound—good. Switch films What they do: - Fill the gap between top and bottom housing to reduce wobble and housing movement. - Often tightens sound (less “plasticky resonance”), can make it slightly deeper and more consistent. - Can improve feel by reducing stem wobble, especially noticeable on tactiles. Do U4Ts need films? - Typically: not really. One of the selling points is tight housings and good tolerances. Many people run them unfilmed and they feel solid. - Exception: If your batch has noticeable wobble or you’re extremely sensitive, films can still help, but it’s not a default necessity. Do Holy Pandas benefit? - Often: yes, depending on the specific Holy Panda variant. Some have more housing play than premium tight-tolerance switches. Films can reduce wobble and can make the sound more consistent. - But: Films can also increase friction slightly if tolerances get too tight, especially if the switch is already snug. This is more of a risk with some tactiles where any added friction can affect return feel. If you want to minimize rabbit holes: - Skip films on U4T. - For Drop Holy Pandas, you can try a small batch: film 10 switches and compare. If it’s subtle, skip it. Since you’re willing to lube, the biggest wins are: - Spring lube (always). - Light stem rail lube. - Avoiding the tactile legs. Do you plan to buy a switch tester, or are you choosing blind based on descriptions?

I might buy a small sample pack, but I’m worried testers don’t represent the real feel in a full board. Is that true? Also, can you talk about PC plate vs FR4 vs aluminum in terms of tactile feel? I picked PC but I’m not 100% sure.

Switch testers are useful, but they’re imperfect—your concern is valid. Why testers can mislead - Mounting: A single switch in acrylic or a small metal plate doesn’t replicate how a switch feels in a full plate/PCB assembly with gaskets and case damping. - Acoustics: Sound in a tester is almost meaningless compared to a full board. - Keycap differences: Testers often use random caps; your thick PBT Cherry caps will change leverage and sound. - Stabilized keys: Testers don’t tell you anything about space/enter behavior. Why testers still help - They let you feel tactile shape (sharp vs rounded), basic weight, and stem wobble. - They can prevent obvious mismatches (e.g., “I hate sharp tactiles”). Plate material differences (feel + sound) PC (polycarbonate) - Feel: Softer, more flex. Can make bottom-out feel less harsh and tactility feel slightly more rounded. - Sound: Tends to mute high frequencies; deeper and less metallic. - Good for: Deep, comfortable builds; “cushioned premium” goals. FR4 (fiberglass/epoxy, like PCB material) - Feel: Stiffer than PC but not as stiff/ringy as aluminum. Often feels “crisp” without being harsh. - Sound: Balanced—can be lower than aluminum but with more clarity than PC. - Good for: People who want definition and consistent feel without too much flex. Aluminum - Feel: Stiff, direct. Tactiles feel crisp and immediate; bottom-out can be firm. - Sound: More “clack” potential and can introduce metallic resonance depending on case. - Good for: Crisp, responsive typing; not ideal if you’re chasing soft/deep unless the case is heavily damped. Given your goals: - PC is a sensible pick. It’s forgiving and tends to steer away from harshness. - If you later feel it’s too muted or flexy, FR4 is the typical “adds clarity” step without going full aluminum. One more variable: plate thickness and mounting style can matter as much as material. A thick PC plate can feel less flexy than a thin FR4, etc. Do you prefer a bit of flex (“bouncy”) or a firm keybed (“solid”)?
Slight flex is fine, but I don’t want it to feel like a trampoline. More “controlled.” Also, I’ve heard Holy Pandas can have leaf ping. Is that real, and can it be fixed? I hate ping.

Leaf ping is real on some tactile switches, including some Holy Panda variants, though it’s not universal. What “leaf ping” is - The “leaf” is the metal contact inside the switch that the stem interacts with. - In some switches, that metal part resonates when struck or when it snaps back, creating a high-pitched “ting” that can cut through even a deep-sounding build. Why Holy Pandas can ping - Strong tactility means the stem is interacting more aggressively with the leaf. - Tolerances and leaf design can make resonance more likely. - Some batches/brands are simply more prone. How to fix or reduce it 1) Spring lube - Sometimes what people call leaf ping is actually spring ping. Lubing springs with 105 oil or bag lubing often eliminates a lot of “ting.” 2) Proper switch lube (carefully) - Light lube on stem rails reduces scratch and can reduce some high-frequency noise. - Avoid lubing the leaf directly, but a very tiny amount near (not on) the contact area can sometimes damp resonance—this is tricky and not generally recommended unless you know what you’re doing, because it can affect electrical contact or tactility if you overdo it. 3) Films - Can reduce housing resonance and alter frequencies, making ping less noticeable. Not a guaranteed fix for leaf ping, but can help. 4) Board-level damping - Plate foam + case foam can reduce perceived ping by absorbing higher frequencies and reducing amplification. - Gasket mount already helps. If you truly hate ping, U4T tends to be less “pingy” in many builds, partly because of housing design and because people often run them well-lubed. Also, be aware: sometimes “ping” comes from the case or plate, not the switch. A metal weight or loose screws can ring. So troubleshooting matters. If you pick Holy Pandas, I’d recommend: - Lube springs first, test for ping. - If ping remains, lube stems/housing lightly. - Only then consider films. Are you planning to hand-lube every switch, or use a more minimal approach?
Hand-lube, but I want an efficient process. Probably 90 switches. Can you outline an efficient workflow, and what tools matter? Also, should I break in switches before lubing?

Efficient hand-lubing is mostly about setup, consistency, and not overthinking each switch. Tools that matter - Switch opener (MX style): saves a ton of time and prevents damage. - Fine brush (size 00 or similar): for controlled application. - Tweezers: for springs and small parts. - Lube station (optional but helpful): keeps parts organized. - Stem holder (optional): speeds up handling. - Small containers/bags: for bag-lubing springs. - Good lighting: prevents over-lubing. Lubing choices for your likely paths - Linears (Gateron Yellow): 205g0 on stem/housing, 105 oil on springs. - Tactiles (U4T / Holy Panda): 3203 or 3204 on stem rails/housing; 105 on springs. Efficient workflow (90 switches) 1) Prep - Clean workspace, lay out tools. - Decide on lube and commit (switching lubes mid-way creates inconsistency). 2) Open all switches first (batching) - Pop tops off and separate: tops, bottoms, stems, springs. - Keep parts grouped by switch to avoid mixing if you care about consistency (usually fine within same model). 3) Bag-lube springs (fast win) - Put springs in a small zip bag. - Add 2–4 drops of 105 oil (or a tiny smear of 205g0 if donut dipping). - Shake and massage gently until coated. - Let sit while you lube stems/housings. 4) Lube bottoms (optional but can be efficient) - For linears: light coat on the rails where stem slides. - For tactiles: same, but keep it light. 5) Lube stems - Linears: rails + optionally the front/back faces lightly; avoid over-lubing the pole. - Tactiles: rails only, very light; avoid tactile legs. 6) Reassemble - Spring into bottom housing. - Stem in (correct orientation). - Top housing on. 7) Quality check - Press each switch a few times. Listen for crunch/ping, feel for sluggishness. - If something feels sticky, it’s usually too much lube on the pole or rails. Break-in before lubing? - Pros: Some switches get smoother after a few thousand actuations. Breaking in stock then lubing can yield a very smooth end result. - Cons: It’s time-consuming. Also, lubing first often makes break-in less necessary because lube masks initial scratch. - Practical recommendation: For most builds, skip formal break-in. Lube them, then normal use will break them in. If you’re extremely sensitive and chasing peak smoothness on linears, you can break in, but it’s a lot of work. For tactiles, break-in can slightly change the crispness; some people prefer fresh crisp tactility, others like it mellowed. If you want “rounded but premium,” lubing with a thinner tactile lube already gets you there. What’s your priority: maximum smoothness, or preserving tactile crispness?
Preserve tactile character, but remove scratch and noise. So probably 3203? Also, what about “factory lubed” switches—should I avoid them? Some U4Ts are pre-lubed.

If you want to preserve tactile character while smoothing scratch, 3203 is a good choice. It’s thin enough to keep definition, especially if applied lightly. Factory lubed switches - Not inherently bad. In fact, many modern switches come with decent factory lube compared to older standards. - The problem is consistency: some switches are evenly lubed, others have uneven blobs or dry spots, which can create inconsistent feel across keys. U4T factory lube - Many U4Ts are lightly lubed from the factory (depending on vendor/batch). - Often it’s “good enough” for a lot of people, especially if you don’t want to spend hours. - If you’re sensitive to inconsistency, you can: 1) Use them stock, accept minor variation. 2) “Touch-up lube” only the scratchy ones (time-efficient). 3) Clean and relube everything (most consistent, most work). Should you avoid factory lubed? - If you enjoy the build process and want uniformity, hand-lubing yourself is still the gold standard. - If you want to reduce workload, factory lubed U4Ts plus spring lube can be a strong compromise. One caution: mixing factory lube with your own can sometimes create uneven thickness if you just add more on top. If the factory lube is already present, the best approach is usually: - Either leave it and only lube springs, - Or clean stems/housings (tedious) and relube. If you’re aiming for efficient but high quality: try a small batch of stock U4Ts, see if you like them. If they’re already smooth and consistent enough, you can save a lot of time. Are you comfortable desoldering or are you building hot-swap? That affects how risky experimentation is.
Hot-swap. So I can change switches later. That’s partly why I’m overthinking. Another question: how much do keycaps affect switch feel? People say different profiles change tactility.

Keycaps affect feel more than most people expect, mainly through leverage, contact point, and acoustics. How keycaps change perceived feel 1) Profile height and top shape (leverage) - Taller profiles (SA, MT3) place your finger higher above the switch stem, increasing leverage. That can make the switch feel slightly lighter and can make tactility feel more pronounced or “bigger” because your finger applies force with a longer moment arm. - Lower profiles (Cherry, OEM) feel more direct and can make tactility feel a bit tighter/controlled. 2) Sculpt and typing angle - Sculpted rows change finger approach angle, affecting how you push the key (more forward/back). That can change how you perceive bump sharpness. 3) Keycap material and thickness (acoustics + inertia) - Thick PBT: tends to damp higher frequencies and add a deeper sound. Also slightly more mass can change how the key returns and how bottom-out feels. - Thin ABS: often brighter and can emphasize clack; also can feel “livelier.” - But: acoustics dominate your perception; sometimes you interpret a deeper sound as “softer feel.” Do different caps change tactility? - The switch’s tactile event is mechanical and doesn’t change, but your perception of it can change due to leverage and sound. - Cherry profile thick PBT is a good “controlled” baseline—unlikely to exaggerate tactility in a weird way. Since you’re on hot-swap, you can iterate: - Start with a switch that’s close to your target feel (U4T seems aligned). - Then tune sound with foam and caps. - If gaming feels off, you can try slightly lighter springs or a different tactile with a less aggressive bump. Are you considering any specific tactile alternatives besides U4T and Holy Panda, or do you want to keep the shortlist tight?

Keep it tight. Let’s say it’s between U4T and Drop Holy Panda, with a wildcard of Gateron Yellow if I decide tactiles aren’t for me. Can you give a decision framework? Like if I care about X, choose Y.

Here’s a practical decision framework for those three, based on your stated goals (deep sound, rounded tactile, not obnoxious, mixed typing/gaming, PC plate gasket). If you care most about “premium tactile satisfaction without harshness” → U4T - Why: Strong tactile but generally more rounded and deep-sounding than Holy Panda. Less risk of “curb” feel and less likely to be annoyingly poppy in your build. - Best if: You want a tactile board you can type on all day with minimal regret. - Tuning: 62g spring (or stock if it matches), 3203 light lube, foam as needed. If you care most about “iconic poppy tactile character” and don’t mind loudness → Drop Holy Panda - Why: Big, distinctive tactile event and sound. Fun, dramatic. - Best if: You want the tactile to be the main feature and you like crisp/snappy feedback. - Risks: Possible leaf/spring ping, more likely to feel loud/attention-grabbing, more fatigue risk if you type heavy-handed. - Tuning: Spring lube is mandatory; 3203/3204 careful lube; consider films if wobble bothers you; foam to control volume. If you care most about “gaming ease + smoothness + deep creamy sound” → Gateron Yellow (milky top) - Why: Linears are effortless for rapid repeats and can sound excellent in a damped gasket/PC build. - Best if: You suspect you might not like strong tactility or you prioritize gaming feel. - Risks: You might miss tactile satisfaction for typing; you may bottom out more because there’s no bump cue. - Tuning: 205g0 + spring oil; films optional. Tie-breakers (quick questions) 1) Do you want the switch to “announce” itself with every keypress? - Yes → Holy Panda - No, I want satisfying but calmer → U4T 2) Are you sensitive to sharp bumps? - Yes → U4T - No / I like crisp snap → Holy Panda 3) Are you primarily playing fast-twitch games with lots of repeated key taps? - Yes → Yellow (or lighter tactile) - Mixed / mostly typing → U4T (or HP if you love it) Given everything you said, U4T is the most aligned default. If you want to hedge, buy 10–20 of each (U4T and HP), put them in the home row and WASD area temporarily, and test in your actual board (hot-swap makes this easy). The board context matters more than any tester.
That makes sense. If I do that hybrid test in the same board, will mixing switches mess with acoustics enough to confuse me? Like, won’t the board sound weird?

It will sound a bit uneven, but for evaluation it’s still useful—especially for feel. How to make the test less confusing - Focus on feel first: Do slow presses and controlled typing on specific keys you know (home row). Feel differences are easier to isolate than sound. - Compare similar positions: Put U4T and Holy Panda on symmetrical keys (e.g., F and J, D and K) so plate/case resonance differences are minimized. - Use the same keycap type and row: Don’t compare a top-row key to a home-row key if the cap sculpt differs. - For sound: Do “single key” taps and short phrases, but understand the board will have mixed timbre. You’re listening for relative pitch and character, not the absolute “board sound.” Also, in a gasket mount with foam, the board tends to homogenize differences slightly, which can actually help you decide: if two switches sound more similar than expected, then feel becomes the deciding factor. If you find both tactiles acceptable, you might then decide based on: - Consistency and QC (U4T often wins), - Availability and price, - How much tuning effort you want.
Last thing: can you explain “pre-travel” and “post-travel” in switches? Does that matter for tactiles? And do Holy Pandas have issues there?

Pre-travel and post-travel are terms about movement relative to actuation. - Pre-travel: How far the key moves before the switch actuates (registers). In MX-style switches, this is often around 2.0 mm, but it can vary by design and by how the tactile event is positioned. - Post-travel: How much travel remains after actuation until bottom-out (e.g., if total travel is 4.0 mm and actuation is at 2.0 mm, post-travel is ~2.0 mm). Why it matters Typing feel: - If pre-travel is short, the key feels more immediate/responsive. - If pre-travel is long, it can feel a bit “floaty” before it registers. Gaming feel: - Shorter pre-travel can feel faster for actuation, but can also increase accidental presses if you rest fingers heavily. - Post-travel affects how much “cushion” you have after actuation; some people like extra post-travel because it feels less abrupt, others prefer shorter travel for rapid inputs. Tactiles specifically - The position of the tactile bump relative to actuation matters a lot: - If the bump occurs before actuation and then drops, you can get a sensation of the key “giving way” before it registers, which can feel odd to some. - If the bump aligns closely with actuation, the tactile event feels like a confirmation of the press. - Many popular tactiles aim to have the tactile event near the top and close to actuation for a decisive feel. Holy Pandas - They typically have a strong, early tactile event. Many people feel it as an immediate bump near the top. - Whether that aligns perfectly with actuation depends on the specific implementation and tolerances. Some users perceive it as “bump then press continues,” which is normal, but the strong bump can dominate perception so much that actuation details feel secondary. - They don’t have a universal “pre-travel problem,” but if you’re sensitive, you might notice that the bump is so prominent that the rest of the travel feels like a drop-through. If you want a tactile that feels like “press → bump/confirmation → continue,” you’ll probably like U4T’s style more than HP’s sharper pop, but it’s subjective. If you share which 75% kit you’re using (just the mounting style and general construction—no personal context needed), it could help predict how sharp or muted these switches will feel and sound in that specific case.
